metadata.note: Questions are curated based on Infosys coding rounds and interview experiences. Options are structured for MCQ-style assessments. No duplicates are present.

questions.title: Two Sum
questions.problem_statement: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers that add up to target.
options: A) O(n^2) time, O(1) space using nested loops
B) O(n) time, O(n) space using a hash map
C) O(n log n) time, O(1) space using two pointers after sorting
D) O(n) time, O(1) space using two pointers
questions.correct_answer: B) O(n) time, O(n) space using a hash map
questions.explanation: A hash map provides O(1) lookup for the complement of each element, achieving O(n) time complexity.

questions.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
questions.problem_statement: You are given an array prices where prices[i] is the price of a given stock on the ith day. Find the maximum profit you can achieve.
options: A) Keep track of minimum price so far and calculate max profit at each step
B) Sort the array and return difference between max and min
C) Use Kadane's algorithm on price differences
D) Both A and C are valid approaches
questions.correct_answer: D) Both A and C are valid approaches
questions.explanation: Tracking min price so far (O(n)) and applying Kadane's on differences (max subarray) are both standard solutions.

questions.title: Maximum Subarray (Kadane's Algorithm)
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer array nums, find the contiguous subarray with the largest sum and return its sum.
options: A) O(n^3) using brute force subarray enumeration
B) O(n^2) using prefix sums
C) O(n) using Kadane's algorithm with currentMax and globalMax
D) O(n log n) using divide and conquer
questions.correct_answer: C) O(n) using Kadane's algorithm with currentMax and globalMax
questions.explanation: Kadane's algorithm dynamically tracks the maximum sum ending at each position in O(n) time.

questions.title: Product of Array Except Self
questions.problem_statement: Return an array output such that output[i] is equal to the product of all elements of nums except nums[i]. Solve without division.
options: A) Brute force: multiply all elements and divide by nums[i]
B) Use prefix and suffix product arrays in O(n) time
C) Sort the array and use two pointers
D) Use a binary indexed tree
questions.correct_answer: B) Use prefix and suffix product arrays in O(n) time
questions.explanation: Left and right passes compute products before and after each index without using division.

questions.title: 3Sum
questions.problem_statement: Find all triplets in the array that sum to zero. The solution must not contain duplicate triplets.
options: A) O(n^3) brute force with a set to remove duplicates
B) Sort the array and use two-pointer technique for each element
C) Use a hash map to store all pairs and find complements
D) Both B and C are valid but B is more space-efficient
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C are valid but B is more space-efficient
questions.explanation: Sorting + two pointers gives O(n^2) time and O(1) extra space, while hash map gives O(n^2) time and O(n) space.

questions.title: Container With Most Water
questions.problem_statement: Given n non-negative integers representing heights, find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container that holds the most water.
options: A) Check all pairs (O(n^2)) and compute area
B) Two-pointer approach starting from ends and moving the shorter line inward
C) Sort the array and use binary search
D) Greedy approach always moving the left pointer
questions.correct_answer: B) Two-pointer approach starting from ends and moving the shorter line inward
questions.explanation: The two-pointer approach achieves O(n) time by gradually narrowing the width while seeking taller boundaries.

questions.title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
questions.problem_statement: There is an integer array nums sorted in ascending order with distinct values, then rotated at an unknown pivot. Given target, return its index or -1.
options: A) Linear search O(n)
B) Modified binary search checking which half is properly sorted
C) Find pivot using binary search then binary search in appropriate half
D) Both B and C are valid approaches
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C are valid approaches
questions.explanation: Either find pivot first (two binary searches) or adapt binary search to directly handle the rotated property.

questions.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
questions.problem_statement: Given a string s, find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.
options: A) Generate all substrings O(n^3) and check for duplicates
B) Sliding window with a set to track characters in current window
C) Sliding window with a hash map to store last index of each character
D) Both B and C achieve O(n) time
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C achieve O(n) time
questions.explanation: Set tracks presence, map tracks last index, both enable the sliding window to expand/shrink efficiently.

questions.title: Longest Palindromic Substring
questions.problem_statement: Given a string s, return the longest palindromic substring in s.
options: A) Generate all substrings and check palindrome O(n^3)
B) Expand around center technique O(n^2)
C) Dynamic programming O(n^2) time and space
D) Manacher's algorithm O(n) time
questions.correct_answer: D) Manacher's algorithm O(n) time
questions.explanation: While expand around center (O(n^2)) is common, Manacher's algorithm solves it in linear time.

questions.title: Valid Parentheses
questions.problem_statement: Given a string s containing just the characters '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid.
options: A) Use a counter for each bracket type independently
B) Use a stack to push opening brackets and pop when closing brackets match
C) Replace all matching pairs repeatedly until empty
D) Both B and C are correct but B is more efficient
questions.correct_answer: B) Use a stack to push opening brackets and pop when closing brackets match
questions.explanation: A stack maintains the order of brackets, ensuring the most recent opening matches the current closing.

questions.title: Reverse a Linked List
questions.problem_statement: Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse the list and return its head.
options: A) Create a new reversed list by iterating from head to tail
B) Use three pointers (prev, curr, next) to reverse links iteratively
C) Recursive approach: reverse the rest and adjust pointers
D) Both B and C are valid approaches
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C are valid approaches
questions.explanation: Both iterative (prev-curr-next) and recursive solutions correctly reverse a linked list in O(n) time.

questions.title: Detect Cycle in Linked List
questions.problem_statement: Given head of a linked list, determine if the linked list has a cycle.
options: A) Use a hash set to store visited nodes O(n) time and space
B) Floyd's Tortoise and Hare algorithm with slow and fast pointers
C) Reverse the list and check if head matches tail
D) Both A and B are correct
questions.correct_answer: D) Both A and B are correct
questions.explanation: Hash set is simpler but O(n) space; Floyd's algorithm detects cycles in O(n) time with O(1) space.

questions.title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
questions.problem_statement: Merge two sorted linked lists and return it as a new sorted list.
options: A) Append both lists and sort O(n log n)
B) Two-pointer merge similar to merge sort O(m+n)
C) Insert all nodes of second list into first sequentially
D) Reverse both lists then merge
questions.correct_answer: B) Two-pointer merge similar to merge sort O(m+n)
questions.explanation: Comparing heads and advancing the smaller node yields a linear-time merge similar to merge sort.

questions.title: Remove Nth Node From End of List
questions.problem_statement: Given the head of a linked list, remove the nth node from the end and return its head.
options: A) Compute length, then traverse to len-n-1 and remove
B) Use two pointers with a gap of n nodes
C) Reverse the list, remove nth from start, reverse back
D) Both A and B are valid
questions.correct_answer: D) Both A and B are valid
questions.explanation: One-pass with two pointers (gap technique) or two-pass with length calculation both solve the problem.

questions.title: Add Two Numbers
questions.problem_statement: You are given two non-empty linked lists representing two non-negative integers. The digits are stored in reverse order. Add the two numbers and return the sum as a linked list.
options: A) Convert lists to integers, add, convert back
B) Traverse both lists simultaneously with carry propagation
C) Pad shorter list with zeros, then add digit by digit
D) Both B and C are correct approaches
questions.correct_answer: B) Traverse both lists simultaneously with carry propagation
questions.explanation: Simultaneous traversal with carry handling is the standard O(max(m,n)) solution.

questions.title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
questions.problem_statement: Given the root of a binary tree, return the level order traversal of its nodes' values (i.e., left to right, level by level).
options: A) Use recursion with a depth parameter
B) Use BFS with a queue to process nodes level by level
C) Use DFS and store nodes in a hash map keyed by level
D) Both A and B are valid approaches
questions.correct_answer: B) Use BFS with a queue to process nodes level by level
questions.explanation: BFS naturally processes nodes level by level; recursion can also work with level tracking.

questions.title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
questions.problem_statement: Given the root of a binary tree, return its maximum depth (number of nodes along longest path from root to leaf).
options: A) BFS: count levels until queue becomes empty
B) DFS recursion: 1 + max(depth(left), depth(right))
C) Iterative DFS using stack with depth tracking
D) All of the above
questions.correct_answer: D) All of the above
questions.explanation: All three approaches correctly compute tree depth: BFS counts levels, DFS recursion uses post-order, and iterative DFS tracks depth on stack.

questions.title: Validate Binary Search Tree
questions.problem_statement: Given the root of a binary tree, determine if it is a valid binary search tree (BST).
options: A) Check that left < root < right for every node (naive, fails for deeper violations)
B) In-order traversal and check if values are strictly increasing
C) Recursive validation with min/max bounds passed down
D) Both B and C are correct
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C are correct
questions.explanation: In-order traversal gives sorted order for BST; bounds-passing recursion ensures left subtree values are within range [min, root.val].

questions.title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Search Tree
questions.problem_statement: Given a BST and two nodes p and q, find the lowest common ancestor (LCA).
options: A) Find paths from root to p and q then compare
B) Exploit BST property: LCA is node where p and q are on different sides or one equals current
C) Use parent pointers stored in a hash map
D) Both A and B are valid but B is optimal
questions.correct_answer: B) Exploit BST property: LCA is node where p and q are on different sides or one equals current
questions.explanation: BST property allows O(h) traversal without extra space: if both greater, go right; both smaller, go left; else current is LCA.

questions.title: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum
questions.problem_statement: A path is any sequence of nodes where each pair of adjacent nodes has an edge. Find the maximum path sum (path may start and end at any node).
options: A) Check all possible paths O(n^2)
B) DFS returning max gain from node to leaf while tracking global max that may split at node
C) BFS with priority queue
D) Use Kadane's algorithm on flattened tree
questions.correct_answer: B) DFS returning max gain from node to leaf while tracking global max that may split at node
questions.explanation: For each node, compute max gain from left and right, update global max with left+right+node.val, and return node.val + max(leftGain, rightGain).

questions.title: Number of Islands
questions.problem_statement: Given an m x n 2D binary grid where '1' represents land and '0' water, count the number of islands (surrounded by water horizontally/vertically).
options: A) For each '1', perform DFS/BFS to mark all connected land as visited
B) Use union-find to connect adjacent land cells
C) Scan grid and flood fill when encountering unvisited land
D) All of the above
questions.correct_answer: D) All of the above
questions.explanation: DFS/BFS flood fill, union-find (disjoint set), and iterative scanning with marking all work. Choose based on constraints.

questions.title: Clone Graph
questions.problem_statement: Given a reference of a node in a connected undirected graph, return a deep copy (clone) of the graph.
options: A) BFS using queue and mapping from original to clone nodes
B) DFS with recursion and hash map tracking visited nodes
C) Copy adjacency lists directly without traversing
D) Both A and B are valid
questions.correct_answer: D) Both A and B are valid
questions.explanation: Both BFS and DFS with a hash map ensure each node is cloned once and all edges are recreated.

questions.title: Course Schedule (Cycle Detection in Directed Graph)
questions.problem_statement: There are numCourses courses labeled 0 to numCourses-1. Given prerequisites [a,b] meaning b->a, determine if you can finish all courses (no cycle).
options: A) Detect cycle using DFS with three states (unvisited, visiting, visited)
B) Use Kahn's algorithm (topological sort with indegree tracking)
C) Run Floyd-Warshall and check for self loops
D) Both A and B are correct
questions.correct_answer: D) Both A and B are correct
questions.explanation: DFS with state tracking detects back edges; Kahn's algorithm detects cycles if nodes remaining after processing.

questions.title: Climbing Stairs
questions.problem_statement: You are climbing a staircase with n steps. You can climb 1 or 2 steps at a time. Find number of distinct ways to reach the top.
options: A) Fibonacci: dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]
B) Combinatorics: sum of C(n-k, k) for k from 0 to n/2
C) Recursion with memoization
D) All of the above
questions.correct_answer: D) All of the above
questions.explanation: All approaches yield the Fibonacci solution: DP bottom-up, recursion with memoization, or combinatorics counting 2-step placements.

questions.title: Coin Change
questions.problem_statement: Given coins of different denominations and a total amount, return the fewest number of coins to make that amount. If impossible, return -1.
options: A) Greedy: always use largest coin first (works only for canonical systems)
B) DP: dp[i] = min over coins of (dp[i-coin] + 1)
C) BFS on amount space
D) Both B and C are valid
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C are valid
questions.explanation: DP bottom-up solves unbounded knapsack; BFS on states (amount) also finds shortest path. Greedy fails for non-canonical systems.

questions.title: Longest Common Subsequence
questions.problem_statement: Given two strings text1 and text2, return the length of their longest common subsequence.
options: A) Brute force: generate all subsequences of both O(2^(m+n))
B) DP: if chars match, 1 + dp[i-1][j-1]; else max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])
C) Recursion with memoization (top-down DP)
D) Both B and C are correct
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C are correct
questions.explanation: Standard 2D DP provides O(mn) solution; top-down memoization follows the same recurrence.

questions.title: House Robber
questions.problem_statement: You are a professional robber planning to rob houses along a street. Adjacent houses have security systems. Find max amount without robbing two adjacent houses.
options: A) dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i])
B) Use two variables to track previous two states (space optimization)
C) Greedy: rob every other house
D) Both A and B are correct
questions.correct_answer: D) Both A and B are correct
questions.explanation: DP recurrence with space optimization using two variables gives O(n) time O(1) space. Greedy fails for cases like [2,1,1,2].

questions.title: Jump Game
questions.problem_statement: Given array nums where each element represents max jump length, determine if you can reach the last index.
options: A) Greedy: track max reachable index, return true if maxReach >= last index
B) DP from right: dp[i] = any dp[i+j] for j in 1..nums[i]
C) BFS on indices
D) All of the above
questions.correct_answer: D) All of the above
questions.explanation: Greedy O(n) is optimal; DP O(n^2) and BFS O(n^2) also work but less efficient.

questions.title: Permutations (Backtracking)
questions.problem_statement: Given an array nums of distinct integers, return all possible permutations.
options: A) Use next_permutation repeatedly after sorting (library approach)
B) Backtracking with used boolean array
C) Backtracking by swapping elements in-place
D) Both B and C are valid
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C are valid
questions.explanation: Both approaches generate all permutations: used-array tracks selected elements; swap-based swaps back after recursion.

questions.title: Generate Parentheses
questions.problem_statement: Given n pairs of parentheses, generate all combinations of well-formed parentheses.
options: A) Use recursion adding '(' if open < n and ')' if close < open
B) Catalan number: count = C(2n, n)/(n+1) but generation needs backtracking
C) BFS on string building
D) Both A and C are correct
questions.correct_answer: A) Use recursion adding '(' if open < n and ')' if close < open
questions.explanation: Standard backtracking maintains open and close counts, ensuring never close > open.

questions.title: Letter Combinations of a Phone Number
questions.problem_statement: Given a string containing digits 2-9 inclusive, return all possible letter combinations that the number could represent (like old phone keypad).
options: A) BFS building combinations level by level
B) DFS backtracking mapping each digit to letters
C) Iterative product using reduce
D) All of the above
questions.correct_answer: D) All of the above
questions.explanation: All three approaches generate the cartesian product of letter sets for each digit.

questions.title: Subsets (Power Set)
questions.problem_statement: Given an integer array nums of unique elements, return all possible subsets (the power set).
options: A) Cascading: start with [[]], for each num add num to existing subsets
B) Backtracking with recursion choosing to include/exclude each element
C) Bitmask: iterate from 0 to 2^n-1 and use bits to select elements
D) All of the above
questions.correct_answer: D) All of the above
questions.explanation: All three common approaches work: iterative cascading, recursion with include/exclude, and bit manipulation.

questions.title: Word Search (Matrix)
questions.problem_statement: Given an m x n board of characters and a word, determine if the word exists in the grid. Adjacent cells horizontally or vertically, cannot reuse same cell.
options: A) DFS from each cell, mark visited, backtrack
B) BFS with state tracking visited path
C) Build trie of all words (for multiple words) then traverse
D) Both A and C are relevant for single-word vs multi-word cases
questions.correct_answer: D) Both A and C are relevant for single-word vs multi-word cases
questions.explanation: For single word, standard DFS backtracking works. For multiple words, a Trie + DFS is optimal.

questions.title: Sort Colors (Dutch National Flag)
questions.problem_statement: Given an array nums with values 0,1,2 representing colors red, white, blue, sort them in-place.
options: A) Count frequencies and overwrite array O(n) with O(1) extra space
B) Two-pass: move all 0s to front, then all 2s to back
C) One-pass three pointers (low, mid, high) swapping
D) Both A and C are correct
questions.correct_answer: D) Both A and C are correct
questions.explanation: Counting sort works but needs two passes; Dutch national flag (one-pass three pointers) is the optimal single-pass solution.

questions.title: Find the Duplicate Number
questions.problem_statement: Given an array of n+1 integers where each integer is between 1 and n inclusive, there is one duplicate number. Find it without modifying array and using O(1) space.
options: A) Sort array and check adjacent O(n log n)
B) Use hash set O(n) space
C) Floyd's cycle detection (treat array as linked list by value-to-index mapping)
D) Binary search on value range O(n log n)
questions.correct_answer: C) Floyd's cycle detection (treat array as linked list by value-to-index mapping)
questions.explanation: Treat array as function f(i) = nums[i]; duplicate creates cycle; Floyd's finds cycle start O(n) time O(1) space.

questions.title: Next Permutation
questions.problem_statement: Implement next permutation that rearranges numbers into lexicographically next greater permutation. If not possible, rearrange to lowest order.
options: A) Generate all permutations and find next O(n!)
B) Find first decreasing element from right, then swap with next greater from right, then reverse suffix
C) Use library next_permutation
D) Both B and C effectively implement the same algorithm
questions.correct_answer: B) Find first decreasing element from right, then swap with next greater from right, then reverse suffix
questions.explanation: Standard single-pass algorithm: (1) find first a[i] < a[i+1], (2) find a[j] > a[i] from right, (3) swap, (4) reverse suffix.

questions.title: Maximum Product Subarray
questions.problem_statement: Given integer array nums, find contiguous subarray with largest product.
options: A) Kadane's algorithm applied to log of values (fails for negatives)
B) Track both max and min product at each position to handle negatives
C) Brute force O(n^2)
D) Both B and C are correct but B is O(n)
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C are correct but B is O(n)
questions.explanation: Tracking max and min product handles negative numbers by swapping when current element is negative.

questions.title: Rotate Image (Matrix)
questions.problem_statement: You are given an n x n 2D matrix representing an image. Rotate the image by 90 degrees clockwise in-place.
options: A) Create new matrix and copy values (O(n^2) space)
B) Transpose then reverse each row
C) Rotate layers from outer to inner in cycles of 4
D) Both B and C are valid O(1) space approaches
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C are valid O(1) space approaches
questions.explanation: Transpose+reverse works for clockwise rotation; layer-by-layer rotation also works with unit-sized swaps.

questions.title: Spiral Matrix
questions.problem_statement: Given an m x n matrix, return all elements in spiral order.
options: A) DFS with direction changes on boundary hit
B) Four boundaries (top, bottom, left, right) iterating layer by layer
C) Simulate: move right, down, left, up with visited set
D) Both B and C are standard approaches
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C are standard approaches
questions.explanation: Boundary-shrinking method is simpler; directional simulation with visited boolean is also valid.

questions.title: Set Matrix Zeroes
questions.problem_statement: Given an m x n matrix, if an element is 0, set its entire row and column to 0. Do it in-place using constant extra space.
options: A) Create separate arrays to track zero rows/cols O(m+n) space
B) Use first row and first column as markers, with separate flag for first row/col
C) Brute force: for each zero, set row and col (O(n^3) worst case)
D) Both A and B are correct but B uses O(1) space
questions.correct_answer: D) Both A and B are correct but B uses O(1) space
questions.explanation: Leverage first row/col as markers, track if they originally had zeros separately to achieve O(1) space.

questions.title: Implement Trie (Prefix Tree)
questions.problem_statement: Implement a trie with insert, search, and startsWith methods.
options: A) Each node contains children[26] and boolean isEnd
B) Use hash map for children instead of array (more memory efficient for sparse)
C) Store all words in hash set for search O(1), iterate for startsWith O(n)
D) Both A and B are valid implementations
questions.correct_answer: D) Both A and B are valid implementations
questions.explanation: Array-based children gives O(1) lookup by index; hash map is space-efficient for sparse children.

questions.title: Design Add and Search Words Data Structure
questions.problem_statement: Design data structure supporting addWord(word) and search(word) where '.' matches any letter.
options: A) Hash set of words, search brute force O(26^k)
B) Trie with DFS for '.' wildcards
C) Store words by length in hash map, search within bucket
D) Both B and C can work but B is classic
questions.correct_answer: B) Trie with DFS for '.' wildcards
questions.explanation: Trie with DFS recursively explores all children when encountering '.' character during search.

questions.title: Top K Frequent Elements
questions.problem_statement: Given integer array nums and integer k, return the k most frequent elements.
options: A) Count frequencies, sort by frequency O(n log n)
B) Count frequencies, use min-heap of size k O(n log k)
C) Bucket sort: array of lists indexed by frequency O(n)
D) All of the above
questions.correct_answer: D) All of the above
questions.explanation: All three work: sort, heap, or bucket sort (linear time since frequency ≤ n).

questions.title: Find Median from Data Stream
questions.problem_statement: Design a data structure that supports adding integers and finding the median.
options: A) Keep sorted list, insert in O(n) time
B) Two heaps: max-heap for left half, min-heap for right half, rebalance
C) Use order statistic tree (balanced BST)
D) Both B and C work with O(log n) insert
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C work with O(log n) insert
questions.explanation: Two-heap technique (max-heap for lower half, min-heap for upper half) is most common for streaming median.

questions.title: Merge k Sorted Lists
questions.problem_statement: You are given an array of k linked lists, each sorted in ascending order. Merge all into one sorted list.
options: A) Merge one by one: merge list1 with list2, result with list3, etc. O(k*n)
B) Use min-heap of size k, pop and push next O(n log k)
C) Divide and conquer pairwise merge O(n log k)
D) Both B and C achieve O(n log k)
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C achieve O(n log k)
questions.explanation: Min-heap gives O(n log k) time; divide and conquer pairwise merging also gives O(n log k).

questions.title: Sliding Window Maximum
questions.problem_statement: Given array nums and window size k, return array of max values in each sliding window.
options: A) For each window, find max O(n*k)
B) Use max-heap with lazy deletion O(n log k)
C) Use deque maintaining decreasing order O(n)
D) Both B and C work but C is optimal O(n)
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C work but C is optimal O(n)
questions.explanation: Deque stores indices in decreasing order; front holds window max; O(n) time.

questions.title: Trapping Rain Water
questions.problem_statement: Given n non-negative integers representing elevation map, compute total water trapped after rain.
options: A) For each index, find max left and right heights, water = min(leftMax, rightMax) - height[i]
B) Two-pointer approach moving min side inward
C) Monotonic stack approach
D) All of the above
questions.correct_answer: D) All of the above
questions.explanation: Three common solutions: DP arrays O(n) time/space, two pointers O(n) time O(1) space, and monotonic stack.

questions.title: Minimum Window Substring
questions.problem_statement: Given strings s and t, return minimum window substring of s containing all characters of t.
options: A) Generate all substrings O(n^2), check containment O(m)
B) Sliding window: expand right until window contains t, contract left to minimize
C) Use two pointers with frequency maps and count of required chars
D) Both B and C describe the standard O(n) solution
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C describe the standard O(n) solution
questions.explanation: Standard sliding window with character frequency counts and a 'required' counter achieves O(n) time.

questions.title: Longest Consecutive Sequence
questions.problem_statement: Given unsorted array, find length of longest consecutive elements sequence in O(n) time.
options: A) Sort array O(n log n)
B) Use hash set, then for each num, check if num-1 exists to find start of sequence
C) Union-find to connect consecutive numbers
D) Both B and C can achieve O(n) average time
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C can achieve O(n) average time
questions.explanation: Hash set solution checks each number only once O(n) average; union-find also works but more complex.

questions.title: Valid Sudoku
questions.problem_statement: Determine if a 9x9 Sudoku board is valid (rows, cols, 3x3 sub-boxes have unique digits 1-9).
options: A) Use three 2D arrays tracking seen in rows, cols, boxes
B) Use hash set for each row/col/box during iteration
C) Compute box index as (i/3)*3 + j/3
D) All of the above
questions.correct_answer: D) All of the above
questions.explanation: All approaches validate by marking seen numbers; box index formula maps (i,j) to box number.

questions.title: Group Anagrams
questions.problem_statement: Given array of strings, group anagrams together.
options: A) Sort each string and use as key in hash map
B) Count character frequency and use tuple as key
C) Both A and B are valid
D) Sort entire array by string O(n log n)
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B are valid
questions.explanation: Sorted string or frequency tuple both serve as canonical keys for anagram grouping.

questions.title: Subarray Sum Equals K
questions.problem_statement: Given array nums and integer k, return number of subarrays summing to k.
options: A) Generate all subarrays O(n^2)
B) Prefix sum with hash map: store frequency of prefix sums, check prefixSum - k
C) Sliding window (works only for non-negative numbers)
D) Both B and O(n^2) work but B is O(n) for general case
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and O(n^2) work but B is O(n) for general case
questions.explanation: Prefix sum + hash map gives O(n) time; sliding window requires non-negative numbers.

questions.title: Maximal Rectangle
questions.problem_statement: Given binary matrix, find largest rectangle containing only 1's.
options: A) For each row, treat as histogram height and use largest rectangle in histogram algorithm
B) DP tracking heights, left/right boundaries
C) Brute force all rectangles O(m^2 * n^2)
D) Both A and B build on each row's histogram
questions.correct_answer: D) Both A and B build on each row's histogram
questions.explanation: Standard solution: compute heights from top, then apply largest rectangle in histogram for each row.

questions.title: Daily Temperatures
questions.problem_statement: Given array temperatures, return array where answer[i] = days until warmer temperature.
options: A) For each day, search ahead O(n^2)
B) Monotonic decreasing stack storing indices
C) Process from right, maintain next warmer using next greater element pattern
D) Both B and C are stack-based approaches
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C are stack-based approaches
questions.explanation: Monotonic stack from left or processing from right with stack both achieve O(n) time.

questions.title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
questions.problem_statement: Given array of bar heights, find largest rectangle in histogram.
options: A) For each bar, expand left and right O(n^2)
B) Monotonic stack to find left and right smaller boundaries
C) Divide and conquer: find min, recursively solve left/right
D) Both B and C work but stack gives O(n)
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C work but stack gives O(n)
questions.explanation: Monotonic stack finds previous smaller and next smaller elements in O(n) time.

questions.title: Decode String
questions.problem_statement: Given encoded string like '3[a]2[bc]', decode to 'aaabcbc'.
options: A) Use recursion parsing brackets
B) Use two stacks: one for counts, one for strings
C) Use regex replacement repeatedly
D) Both A and B are common solutions
questions.correct_answer: D) Both A and B are common solutions
questions.explanation: Two-stack iterative approach: push counts and strings when encountering '[', pop when encountering ']'.

questions.title: Implement Queue using Stacks
questions.problem_statement: Implement a FIFO queue using two LIFO stacks.
options: A) Push to stack1, pop from stack2 (if empty transfer from stack1)
B) Use only one stack and recursion for pop
C) Both A and B are valid implementations
D) Cannot implement queue with stacks
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B are valid implementations
questions.explanation: Two-stack amortized O(1) push/pop; single stack with recursion achieves same but uses call stack.

questions.title: Min Stack
questions.problem_statement: Design stack that supports push, pop, top, and retrieving minimum element in O(1) time.
options: A) Store (value, currentMin) pairs in each stack node
B) Use two stacks: main stack and min stack
C) Use single stack storing difference from min (value - min)
D) All of the above
questions.correct_answer: D) All of the above
questions.explanation: All three approaches achieve O(1) for all operations with varying space trade-offs.

questions.title: Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation
questions.problem_statement: Evaluate value of expression in Reverse Polish Notation (postfix).
options: A) Use stack, push numbers, pop two on operator, push result
B) Recursive parsing
C) Both A and B work
D) Cannot be done with stack
questions.correct_answer: A) Use stack, push numbers, pop two on operator, push result
questions.explanation: Stack evaluation is straightforward: push operands, pop two when operator encountered, push back result.

questions.title: Basic Calculator
questions.problem_statement: Implement basic calculator to evaluate expression with +, -, (, ) and spaces. No division/multiplication.
options: A) Use stack for signs and parentheses
B) Convert to postfix then evaluate
C) Recursive descent parser
D) All of the above
questions.correct_answer: D) All of the above
questions.explanation: Stack-based single-pass with sign tracking is most common; conversion to postfix also works.

questions.title: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
questions.problem_statement: Design an algorithm to serialize binary tree to string and deserialize back.
options: A) BFS level order with null markers (like '#' for null)
B) Preorder DFS with null markers
C) Both A and B are valid
D) Cannot be done uniquely
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B are valid
questions.explanation: Preorder/level order with null markers uniquely reconstruct tree; BFS often used for compatibility.

questions.title: Invert Binary Tree
questions.problem_statement: Invert a binary tree (mirror swap left and right children).
options: A) DFS: recursively swap left and right, then recurse
B) BFS: level order swapping children
C) Both A and B work
D) Requires O(1) space
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Both recursive DFS and iterative BFS/DFS can swap children to invert the tree.

questions.title: Diameter of Binary Tree
questions.problem_statement: Given root of binary tree, return length of diameter (longest path between any two nodes). Path length = number of edges.
options: A) For each node, compute left height + right height, track max
B) Use DFS returning height while updating global max diameter
C) Both A and B are same approach
D) BFS with node distances
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B are same approach
questions.explanation: DFS post-order: compute height of node, update diameter = max(diameter, leftHeight + rightHeight).

questions.title: Balanced Binary Tree
questions.problem_statement: Determine if binary tree is height-balanced (height difference ≤ 1 for every node).
options: A) Compute height for each node and check difference O(n log n)
B) Return -1 for unbalanced, otherwise return height (single pass)
C) Both A and B work but B is O(n)
D) In-order traversal check
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work but B is O(n)
questions.explanation: Single DFS returns -1 if subtree unbalanced, otherwise returns height; avoids recomputation.

questions.title: Subtree of Another Tree
questions.problem_statement: Given two binary trees root and subRoot, check if subRoot is subtree of root.
options: A) For each node in root, check if trees are identical
B) Serialize both trees and check substring (need to handle nulls uniquely)
C) Both A and B work but A is standard
D) BFS and compare levels
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work but A is standard
questions.explanation: DFS to find matching root, then identical tree check; serialization with unique null markers also works.

questions.title: Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder Traversal
questions.problem_statement: Given preorder and inorder traversal arrays, construct binary tree.
options: A) First element in preorder is root, find in inorder, recursively build left/right
B) Use hash map for inorder indices for O(1) lookup
C) Both A and B combined give O(n) solution
D) Cannot reconstruct uniquely without both traversals
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B combined give O(n) solution
questions.explanation: Root from preorder, split inorder using map, recurse on left/right with updated indices.

questions.title: Word Ladder
questions.problem_statement: Given beginWord, endWord, and wordList, find length of shortest transformation sequence where each step changes one letter.
options: A) BFS from beginWord, generating all possible next words by changing one letter
B) Bidirectional BFS from begin and end for efficiency
C) Both A and B work but B reduces branching factor
D) DFS with backtracking
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work but B reduces branching factor
questions.explanation: Standard BFS finds shortest path; bidirectional BFS reduces search space by meeting in middle.

questions.title: Pacific Atlantic Water Flow
questions.problem_statement: Given m x n matrix of heights, water flows to Pacific (top/left) or Atlantic (bottom/right). Find cells where water can flow to both.
options: A) For each cell, DFS to check reachability O(m*n*(m+n))
B) Multi-source BFS from Pacific and Atlantic borders
C) DFS from borders marking reachable, intersect results
D) Both B and C achieve O(m*n) with reverse flow
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C achieve O(m*n) with reverse flow
questions.explanation: Start from ocean borders and flow inward (higher or equal height), collect cells reachable from both.

questions.title: Graph Valid Tree (LeetCode Premium - concept question)
questions.problem_statement: Given n nodes and edges, determine if the graph forms a valid tree (connected and acyclic).
options: A) Check edges == n-1 and graph is connected (BFS/DFS)
B) Use union-find to detect cycles and ensure single component
C) Both A and B work
D) Check if there's a cycle using DFS
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Conditions: |E| = n-1 AND fully connected (or union-find detects cycles and counts components).

questions.title: Minimum Height Trees
questions.problem_statement: Given undirected tree, find roots that minimize tree height.
options: A) Try all nodes as root and compute heights O(n^2)
B) Leaf trimming (topological removal) until 1-2 nodes remain
C) Find diameter and roots are its middle node(s)
D) Both B and C are equivalent approaches
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C are equivalent approaches
questions.explanation: Repeatedly remove leaves until one/two nodes remain; these are MHT roots (at center of longest path).

questions.title: Cheapest Flights Within K Stops
questions.problem_statement: Given flights (src, dst, price), find cheapest price from src to dst with at most K stops.
options: A) Modified Dijkstra with state (node, stops) in priority queue
B) Bellman-Ford for K+1 iterations
C) BFS with queue (not priority) exploring prices
D) Both A and B are correct
questions.correct_answer: D) Both A and B are correct
questions.explanation: Dijkstra with stop constraint or Bellman-Ford limited to K+1 edges both yield correct minimum cost.

questions.title: Network Delay Time (Dijkstra)
questions.problem_statement: Given network of nodes and travel times, time to broadcast from node k to all nodes (max shortest path).
options: A) Run BFS (unweighted) — incorrect for weighted
B) Dijkstra's algorithm from source k, take max distance
C) Bellman-Ford from source
D) Both B and C are valid for weighted graphs
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C are valid for weighted graphs
questions.explanation: Dijkstra (positive weights) or Bellman-Ford (handles negative but slower) compute shortest paths from source.

questions.title: Reconstruct Itinerary
questions.problem_statement: Given list of airline tickets, reconstruct itinerary in lexical order using all tickets once.
options: A) Hierholzer's algorithm for Eulerian path with priority queue for lexical order
B) DFS backtracking lexical order
C) Both A and B work, A is more efficient
D) Sort tickets and greedy
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, A is more efficient
questions.explanation: Hierholzer's algorithm constructs Eulerian path in O(E log V) with lexical ordering via heap or sorted adjacency.

questions.title: Is Graph Bipartite?
questions.problem_statement: Determine if graph is bipartite (2-colorable).
options: A) BFS/DFS coloring: assign alternating colors, check conflict
B) Union-find with parity tracking
C) Both A and B are valid
D) Check if graph has odd cycle length
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B are valid
questions.explanation: BFS coloring is standard; union-find with parity can also detect odd cycles.

questions.title: Edit Distance (Levenshtein Distance)
questions.problem_statement: Given two strings, find minimum operations (insert, delete, replace) to convert word1 to word2.
options: A) DP[i][j] = min(DP[i-1][j]+1, DP[i][j-1]+1, DP[i-1][j-1] + (word1[i]!=word2[j]))
B) Recursion with memoization
C) Both A and B work with O(mn) time/space
D) Greedy alignment fails
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work with O(mn) time/space
questions.explanation: Standard DP bottom-up or top-down with memoization both compute edit distance.

questions.title: Decode Ways
questions.problem_statement: Given digit string, count ways to decode (A=1, B=2, ..., Z=26).
options: B) Recursion with memoization
C) Both A and B work
D) Greedy fails because of two-digit dependencies
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: DP state depends on single-digit and two-digit validity; can be solved bottom-up or top-down.

questions.title: Unique Paths
questions.problem_statement: Robot moving only right/down from top-left to bottom-right. Count number of paths.
options: A) DP[i][j] = DP[i-1][j] + DP[i][j-1]
B) Combinatorics: choose m-1 moves down from total (m+n-2) steps
C) Both A and B give same result
D) BFS with state counting
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B give same result
questions.explanation: DP O(mn) or binomial coefficient C(m+n-2, m-1) both compute correct number of paths.

questions.title: Unique Paths II (with Obstacles)
questions.problem_statement: Same as Unique Paths but grid has obstacles (1).
options: A) Same DP with obstacle check: if obstacle, dp[i][j] = 0
B) Use 1D DP space optimization
C) Both A and B work
D) Cannot be solved with DP
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: DP recurrence with obstacle handling, can be optimized to O(n) space using single array.

questions.title: Minimum Path Sum
questions.problem_statement: Given m x n grid with non-negative numbers, find path sum from top-left to bottom-right minimizing sum.
options: A) DP[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(DP[i-1][j], DP[i][j-1])
B) Dijkstra on grid
C) Both A and B work but DP is simpler
D) Greedy choosing smaller neighbor fails
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work but DP is simpler
questions.explanation: DP exploits DAG structure; Dijkstra also works but O(mn log mn).

questions.title: Perfect Squares
questions.problem_statement: Find least number of perfect square numbers (1,4,9,...) that sum to n.
options: A) DP: dp[i] = min(dp[i - j*j] + 1) for all j*j ≤ i
B) BFS on remainder space
C) Lagrange's four-square theorem (greedy fails)
D) Both A and B work
questions.correct_answer: D) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: DP O(n√n) solves; BFS from 0 to n also works with O(n) states.

questions.title: Word Break
questions.problem_statement: Given string s and dictionary wordDict, return true if s can be segmented into space-separated dictionary words.
options: A) DP: dp[i] = true if dp[j] and s[j:i] in wordDict for some j
B) BFS on indices
C) Both A and B work
D) Trie + DFS for optimization
questions.correct_answer: D) Trie + DFS for optimization
questions.explanation: DP O(n^2) or BFS; trie improves word lookup but DP remains O(n^2).

questions.title: Partition Equal Subset Sum
questions.problem_statement: Given integer array, determine if it can be partitioned into two subsets with equal sum.
options: A) Target = sum/2; 0/1 knapsack DP
B) Bitmask DP (bitset optimization)
C) Both A and B work
D) Greedy fails
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Standard subset sum DP O(n*target); bitset optimization reduces constant factor.

questions.title: Combination Sum
questions.problem_statement: Given candidates (can reuse) and target, find all unique combinations summing to target.
options: A) Backtracking with recursion, avoid duplicates by starting index ≥ current
B) DP with combination generation
C) Both A and B work but backtracking standard
D) Greedy fails
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work but backtracking standard
questions.explanation: Backtracking with start index prevents permutations; DP can generate but more complex.

questions.title: Combination Sum II (unique candidates, each used once)
questions.problem_statement: Find combinations where each candidate used at most once (array may have duplicates).
options: A) Sort array, backtrack with index+1, skip duplicates
B) Same as combination sum with visited set
C) Both A and B but A sorts and skips adjacent duplicates
D) Greedy by largest first fails
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B but A sorts and skips adjacent duplicates
questions.explanation: Sorting enables duplicate skipping by checking if i > start and candidates[i] == candidates[i-1].

questions.title: Palindrome Partitioning
questions.problem_statement: Partition string such that every substring is palindrome. Return all possible partitions.
options: A) Backtracking with palindrome check (O(n*2^n))
B) Precompute palindrome DP, then backtrack
C) Both A and B
D) Use Manacher's to find palindromes then backtrack
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B
questions.explanation: Precomputing palindrome DP table optimizes palindrome checks to O(1) during backtracking.

questions.title: N-Queens
questions.problem_statement: Place n queens on n×n chessboard so no two attack each other (row, col, diag).
options: A) Backtracking row by row, track occupied columns and diagonals with sets/arrays
B) Bitmask optimization for diagonals and columns
C) Both A and B work
D) Greedy placement fails
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Standard backtracking O(n!); bitmask uses integers to represent column/diagonal occupancy efficiently.

questions.title: Sudoku Solver
questions.problem_statement: Write a program to solve a Sudoku puzzle by filling empty cells.
options: A) Backtracking with constraint checking (row, col, box)
B) Dancing Links (Algorithm X) for exact cover
C) Both A and B work, A is simpler
D) Brute force all 9^(81) possibilities
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, A is simpler
questions.explanation: Standard backtracking finds solution efficiently for 9x9; Dancing Links solves exact cover problems.

questions.title: Find All Numbers Disappeared in an Array
questions.problem_statement: Given array with 1 ≤ a[i] ≤ n, some appear twice, some missing. Return missing numbers.
options: A) Use hash set O(n) space
B) Mark presence by negating value at index nums[i]-1
C) Sort and scan O(n log n)
D) Both A and B work, B uses O(1) extra space
questions.correct_answer: D) Both A and B work, B uses O(1) extra space
questions.explanation: Marking by negation leverages value range [1,n] to track seen numbers without extra space.

questions.title: Move Zeroes
questions.problem_statement: Move all zeros to end while maintaining relative order of non-zero elements.
options: A) Count zeros, create new array
B) Two-pointer: one for last non-zero found, swapping
C) Both A and B work but B is in-place
D) Sort array
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work but B is in-place
questions.explanation: Two-pointer O(n) in-place: pointer tracks last non-zero, swap when encountering non-zero.

questions.title: Missing Number
questions.problem_statement: Given array containing n distinct numbers from 0 to n, find missing number.
options: A) Sum formula: n*(n+1)/2 - sum(nums)
B) XOR: XOR numbers 0..n and XOR all array elements
C) Sorting and binary search
D) Both A and B work in O(n) time O(1) space
questions.correct_answer: D) Both A and B work in O(n) time O(1) space
questions.explanation: Sum approach may overflow for large n; XOR avoids overflow and works similarly.

questions.title: Majority Element
questions.problem_statement: Find element appearing more than ⌊n/2⌋ times. Assume such element exists.
options: A) Sorting: middle index O(n log n)
B) HashMap counting O(n) time O(n) space
C) Boyer-Moore Voting Algorithm O(n) time O(1) space
D) All of the above
questions.correct_answer: D) All of the above
questions.explanation: Boyer-Moore algorithm finds majority with O(1) space by canceling different elements.

questions.title: Single Number
questions.problem_statement: Given non-empty array where every element appears twice except one, find the single one.
options: A) Use hash set O(n) space
B) XOR all elements: duplicates cancel out
C) Sort and scan O(n log n)
D) Both A and B work, B uses O(1) space
questions.correct_answer: D) Both A and B work, B uses O(1) space
questions.explanation: XOR property: a⊕a=0, 0⊕a=a; XOR of all elements isolates the single number.

questions.title: Reverse Bits
questions.problem_statement: Reverse bits of a given 32-bit unsigned integer.
options: A) Iterate through bits, building result bit by bit
B) Use look-up table for byte reversals
C) Both A and B work
D) Cannot be done in O(1) time
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Bit iteration O(32) works; lookup table trades space for speed (byte-level).

questions.title: Number of 1 Bits (Hamming Weight)
questions.problem_statement: Write a function that returns number of '1' bits in unsigned integer.
options: A) Check each bit by shifting
B) n & (n-1) trick to clear lowest set bit
C) Built-in: Integer.bitCount()
D) All of the above
questions.correct_answer: D) All of the above
questions.explanation: n & (n-1) runs in O(number of set bits), faster than checking all bits.

questions.title: Counting Bits
questions.problem_statement: Given n, return array ans of length n+1 where ans[i] is number of 1 bits in i.
options: A) For each i, call hamming weight O(n log n)
B) DP: ans[i] = ans[i>>1] + (i&1)
C) DP: ans[i] = ans[i & (i-1)] + 1
D) Both B and C give O(n) time
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C give O(n) time
questions.explanation: Recurrence based on right shift or clearing lowest set bit both yield O(n) DP.

questions.title: Maximum XOR of Two Numbers in an Array
questions.problem_statement: Find maximum XOR of any two numbers in given array.
options: A) Pairwise check O(n^2)
B) Build binary trie, for each number find max XOR by traversing opposite bits
C) Bitmask greedy O(32*n)
D) Both B and C are equivalent approaches
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C are equivalent approaches
questions.explanation: Trie or bit-prefix set both achieve O(n * bitLength) by greedily choosing opposite bits.

questions.title: Pow(x, n)
questions.problem_statement: Implement pow(x, n) (x raised to power n).
options: A) Loop n times O(n)
B) Fast exponentiation (binary exponentiation) O(log n)
C) Recursive divide and conquer
D) Both B and C are same approach
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C are same approach
questions.explanation: Fast exponentiation: if n even x^n = (x^(n/2))^2, if odd x^n = x * (x^(n-1)).

questions.title: Sqrt(x)
questions.problem_statement: Compute integer square root of non-negative integer x.
options: A) Newton's method (guess = (guess + x/guess)/2)
B) Binary search on [0, x]
C) Built-in: (int)Math.sqrt(x)
D) Both A and B work
questions.correct_answer: D) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Binary search finds largest mid where mid*mid ≤ x; Newton's method converges faster.

questions.title: Divide Two Integers
questions.problem_statement: Divide two integers without using multiplication, division, mod operator. Return quotient.
options: A) Repeated subtraction O(n) slow
B) Exponential search: double divisor until exceeds dividend, subtract and repeat
C) Bit shifting to find largest multiple
D) Both B and C are same approach
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C are same approach
questions.explanation: Compute largest k such that divisor * 2^k ≤ dividend, subtract and continue (O(log^2 n)).

questions.title: Fraction to Recurring Decimal
questions.problem_statement: Given numerator and denominator, return fraction as string with repeating part in parentheses.
options: A) Simulate long division, track remainders in hash map
B) Detect cycle when remainder repeats
C) Both A and B together solve it
D) Use BigDecimal conversion
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B together solve it
questions.explanation: Long division: append floor, track remainder; when remainder repeats, cycle starts.

questions.title: Happy Number
questions.problem_statement: Write algorithm to determine if a number is happy (sum of squares of digits eventually reaches 1).
options: A) Detect cycle using hash set of seen numbers
B) Floyd's cycle detection (slow/fast pointers on digit square sum)
C) Both A and B work
D) Check if number equals 1 or 4 (unhappy cycle)
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Known cycles: 4,16,37,58,89,145,42,20,4 repeats; Floyd's works without extra space.

questions.title: Plus One
questions.problem_statement: Given array of digits representing integer, add one and return resulting array.
options: A) Convert to integer, add one, convert back (overflow risk)
B) Process from rightmost digit with carry propagation
C) Reverse, add one, reverse back
D) Both B and C work
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C work
questions.explanation: Forward carry propagation: if digit becomes 10, set to 0 and carry 1; if carry remains after loop, insert 1 at front.

questions.title: Add Binary
questions.problem_statement: Given two binary strings, return their sum as binary string.
options: A) Convert to integers, add, convert back (overflow risk for long strings)
B) Simulate addition from rightmost with carry
C) Use BigInteger if language supports
D) Both B and standard approach work
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and standard approach work
questions.explanation: Manual bit-by-bit addition from LSB to MSB handles arbitrary length strings.

questions.title: Excel Sheet Column Title
questions.problem_statement: Given integer column number, return corresponding Excel column title (1->A, 28->AB).
options: A) Convert from decimal to base-26 with offset (n-1 modulation)
B) Recursively find remainder (1-indexed adjustment)
C) Both A and B describe same algorithm
D) Lookup table for up to 26, mapping beyond
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B describe same algorithm
questions.explanation: n-1: find remainder, map to char, n = (n-1)/26, repeat.

questions.title: Excel Sheet Column Number
questions.problem_statement: Given Excel column title, return corresponding column number (A=1, AB=28).
options: A) Treat as base-26 number with 1-indexed conversion
B) result = result * 26 + (c - 'A' + 1)
C) Both A and B
D) Reverse and apply powers
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B
questions.explanation: Traverse left to right: multiply result by 26, add current character's value.

questions.title: Palindrome Number
questions.problem_statement: Determine whether integer is palindrome without converting to string.
options: A) Convert to string and compare
B) Reverse half of number: revertNum = revertNum*10 + x%10, x/=10
C) Compare first and last digits by finding divisor
D) Both B and C work without string conversion
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C work without string conversion
questions.explanation: Reverse half: when revertNum >= x, check equality; handles even/odd length.

questions.title: Roman to Integer
questions.problem_statement: Convert Roman numeral string to integer (I=1, V=5, X=10, L=50, C=100, D=500, M=1000).
options: A) Process left to right: if current < next, subtract; else add
B) Use hash map for symbols, iterate with lookahead
C) Both A and B describe same logic
D) Replace subtractive combos then sum
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B describe same logic
questions.explanation: Key rule: if smaller precedes larger, subtract smaller from total, else add.

questions.title: Integer to Roman
questions.problem_statement: Convert integer to Roman numeral.
options: A) Greedy with values and symbols arrays
B) Process each digit with special cases for 4,9,40,90,400,900
C) Both A and B
D) Use modulo and char arrays
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B
questions.explanation: Greedy approach subtracts largest possible symbol value repeatedly using ordered value-symbol pairs.

questions.title: String to Integer (atoi)
questions.problem_statement: Implement atoi to convert string to integer (handle whitespace, sign, overflow).
options: A) Trim spaces, check sign, iterate digits checking overflow
B) Use long for intermediate, clamp to int bounds
C) Both A and B describe standard approach
D) Use regex and try-catch
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B describe standard approach
questions.explanation: Handle sign, skip leading whitespace; for overflow, check before adding digit or use long.

questions.title: Count and Say
questions.problem_statement: Generate nth term of count-and-say sequence (1, 11, 21, 1211, 111221, ...).
options: A) Iterative: for each term, scan and count consecutive digits
B) Recursively generate nth term from n-1
C) Both A and B work
D) Use regular expressions
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Iterative approach builds each term by scanning previous term and counting runs.

questions.title: Multiply Strings
questions.problem_statement: Given two non-negative integers num1 and num2 as strings, return product as string.
options: A) Convert to long and multiply (overflow risk)
B) Simulate multiplication digit by digit with array of length m+n
C) Use Karatsuba algorithm for large strings
D) Both B and C work but B is standard
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C work but B is standard
questions.explanation: Array-based multiplication: place results at indices i+j, i+j+1, handle carry.

questions.title: Reverse Words in a String
questions.problem_statement: Given string with multiple spaces, reverse order of words.
options: A) Split by space, filter empty, reverse, join
B) Two-pass: reverse whole string, then reverse each word
C) Both A and B work
D) Use stack
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Split-join O(n) space; in-place two-pointer reversal O(1) extra space.

questions.title: Simplify Path
questions.problem_statement: Given absolute path for Unix-style file system, simplify it (canonical path).
options: A) Split by '/', use stack: skip '.' and '', on '..' pop, else push
B) Regular expression replacement of patterns
C) Both A and B
D) Use double-ended queue
questions.correct_answer: A) Split by '/', use stack: skip '.' and '', on '..' pop, else push
questions.explanation: Stack tracks valid directories; '..' pops top if exists.

questions.title: Compare Version Numbers
questions.problem_statement: Compare two version strings (1.01 and 1.001 equal).
options: A) Split by '.', parse integers, compare sequentially
B) Two-pointer with digit parsing without splitting
C) Both A and B work
D) Use built-in version compare
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Split-based parsing is simpler; two-pointer avoids extra array allocation.

questions.title: Valid Palindrome II (delete at most one char)
questions.problem_statement: Determine if string can be palindrome after deleting at most one character.
options: A) Check palindrome normally, on mismatch skip left or right recursively
B) Use two pointers with jump once allowed
C) Both A and B describe same approach
D) Generate all substrings removing one char O(n^2)
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B describe same approach
questions.explanation: Two-pointer O(n): on mismatch, try skipping left or right and check rest.

questions.title: Longest Common Prefix
questions.problem_statement: Find longest common prefix string among array of strings.
options: A) Horizontal scanning: compare first string with others reducing prefix
B) Vertical scanning: compare characters column by column
C) Divide and conquer
D) All of the above
questions.correct_answer: D) All of the above
questions.explanation: All three approaches work; vertical scanning avoids building intermediate substrings.

questions.title: Valid Anagram
questions.problem_statement: Determine if two strings are anagrams (characters same with same frequencies).
options: A) Sort both strings and compare
B) Use frequency array of size 26
C) Use hash map counts
D) All of the above
questions.correct_answer: D) All of the above
questions.explanation: All approaches work; frequency array of size 26 is simplest for lowercase letters.

questions.title: First Unique Character in a String
questions.problem_statement: Find first non-repeating character in string and return its index.
options: A) Two passes: count frequencies, second pass find first with count=1
B) Single pass with queue storing candidates
C) Both A and B work
D) Use indexOf and lastIndexOf
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Two-pass count is simplest O(n); queue helps for streaming but not necessary.

questions.title: Ransom Note
questions.problem_statement: Can ransom note be constructed from magazine letters?
options: A) Count magazine frequencies, subtract note frequencies
B) Sort both strings and two-pointer compare
C) Both A and B work
D) Check if note is subsequence of magazine
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Frequency count O(m+n) is simpler; sorting O(n log n) works but slower.

questions.title: Isomorphic Strings
questions.problem_statement: Determine if two strings are isomorphic (mapping pattern from s to t).
options: A) Use two hash maps for s->t and t->s
B) Use single map and set for mapped values
C) Both A and B work
D) Check lengths and pattern indices
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Check both directions to ensure one-to-one mapping; alternative uses array of size 256.

questions.title: Word Pattern
questions.problem_statement: Given pattern and string, determine if string follows same pattern (bijection).
options: A) Split string, map pattern char to word and word to pattern char
B) Use two hash maps for bidirectional mapping
C) Both A and B work
D) Encode both to indices and compare arrays
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Bidirectional mapping ensures different pattern chars don't map to same word.

questions.title: Find the Difference
questions.problem_statement: String t generated by shuffling string s and adding one char. Find added character.
options: A) Count characters with array and subtract
B) XOR all characters of s and t: result is extra char
C) Both A and B work
D) Sort and compare
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: XOR approach cancels common characters; sum difference also works.

questions.title: Merge Intervals
questions.problem_statement: Given array of intervals, merge overlapping intervals.
options: A) Sort intervals by start, then merge if current.end >= next.start
B) Graph approach connecting overlapping intervals
C) Both A and B work, A is simpler
D) Use sweep line
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, A is simpler
questions.explanation: Sorting O(n log n) yields linear merge; intervals are sorted by start.

questions.title: Insert Interval
questions.problem_statement: Insert new interval into sorted non-overlapping intervals and merge if necessary.
options: A) Find insertion position, merge overlapping by updating start/end
B) Add interval to list, sort, and use merge intervals
C) Both A and B work, A is O(n) without sorting
D) Binary search for insertion then merge
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, A is O(n) without sorting
questions.explanation: Single pass O(n): add intervals ending before new.start, merge overlapping, then add remaining.

questions.title: Non-overlapping Intervals
questions.problem_statement: Find minimum number of intervals to remove to make rest non-overlapping.
options: A) Sort by end, greedy take interval with earliest end
B) Sort by start, dynamic programming
C) Both A and B work, A is greedy O(n log n)
D) Weighted interval scheduling
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, A is greedy O(n log n)
questions.explanation: Greedy by earliest end retains maximum non-overlapping intervals; remove count = n - kept.

questions.title: Meeting Rooms II (LeetCode Premium - concept question)
questions.problem_statement: Find minimum number of conference rooms required.
options: A) Sort intervals, use min-heap for end times, push/pop as needed
B) Separate start and end arrays, sweep line: +1 on start, -1 on end
C) Both A and B achieve O(n log n)
D) Greedy by earliest start times
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B achieve O(n log n)
questions.explanation: Heap tracks earliest ending meeting; sweep line tracks simultaneous meetings.

questions.title: Task Scheduler
questions.problem_statement: Given tasks and cooldown n, find least number of CPU units to finish all tasks.
options: A) Greedy: always execute most frequent available task
B) Math formula: (maxFreq-1)*(n+1) + countMaxFreq
C) Both A and B work, B is O(n) time
D) Simulate with queue and cooldown tracking
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, B is O(n) time
questions.explanation: Math formula works when no idle cycle needed; simulation handles edge cases.

questions.title: Minimum Number of Arrows to Burst Balloons
questions.problem_statement: Find minimum arrows to burst all balloons (arrow can pop overlapping intervals).
options: A) Sort by end, shoot arrow at end of first interval, remove overlapping
B) Sort by start, greedy choice of earliest finish
C) Both A and B achieve O(n log n)
D) Maximum overlapping intervals
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B achieve O(n log n)
questions.explanation: Sort by end, shoot at first balloon's end, remove all covering that point, repeat.

questions.title: Valid Parenthesis String (with * wildcard)
questions.problem_statement: String contains '(', ')', and '*' where '*' can be '(' or ')' or empty. Check if valid.
options: A) Two-pass: left to right treating * as '('; right to left treating * as ')'
B) Greedy with low/high range of possible open counts
C) Both A and B work
D) DP O(n^2)
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Low/high range greedy: low=min possible opens, high=max possible opens; never drop below 0.

questions.title: Gas Station
questions.problem_statement: Given gas and cost arrays, find starting gas station index to complete circuit.
options: A) Check each station O(n^2)
B) Greedy: if total gas < total cost return -1; start where tank never negative
C) Two-pointer circular simulation
D) Both B and standard solutions
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and standard solutions
questions.explanation: If total gas >= total cost, solution exists; start from station where cumulative sum minimal.

questions.title: Candy
questions.problem_statement: Distribute minimum candies to children with ratings such that higher rating gets more than neighbor.
options: A) Two-pass: left to right, right to left optimizing
B) One pass with slope tracking (up/down peaks)
C) Both A and B work
D) Sort and assign greedily
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Left pass ensures neighbors with higher rating get more; right pass adjusts for left neighbors.

questions.title: Lemonade Change
questions.problem_statement: Customers pay with $5, $10, $20 for $5 lemonade. Each customer pays exactly $5. Can you give change?
options: A) Keep count of $5 and $10; for $20 use $10+$5 if possible else three $5
B) Always give largest bills first for change
C) Both A and B describe same greedy
D) DP state machine
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B describe same greedy
questions.explanation: Greedy by preferring $10+$5 over three $5 for $20 preserves $5 for future transactions.

questions.title: Queue Reconstruction by Height
questions.problem_statement: Reconstruct queue given [h, k] where k = number of people taller or equal in front.
options: A) Sort by height descending, then by k ascending, insert at index k
B) Sort by height ascending, then by k descending
C) Both A and different approaches
D) Priority queue greedy
questions.correct_answer: A) Sort by height descending, then by k ascending, insert at index k
questions.explanation: Taller people inserted first; inserting at index k places them with exactly k taller/equal in front.

questions.title: Partition Labels
questions.problem_statement: Partition string into as many parts as possible so each letter appears in at most one part.
options: A) Store last occurrence of each char; iterate, track current segment end
B) Greedy: extend segment until reach last occurrence of all chars in segment
C) Both A and B describe same approach
D) Backtracking and DP
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B describe same approach
questions.explanation: O(n) single pass: track last index of each char; expand current segment until i reaches segment end.

questions.title: Jump Game II
questions.problem_statement: Find minimum number of jumps to reach last index.
options: A) DP O(n^2)
B) Greedy BFS: track current max reach, next max reach, count jumps
C) Both A and B work, B is O(n)
D) Dijkstra with steps as weights
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, B is O(n)
questions.explanation: Greedy: at each jump, jump to farthest reachable position from current range.

questions.title: Maximum Subarray Sum with One Deletion
questions.problem_statement: Return maximum sum of contiguous subarray with at most one deletion.
options: A) Track dp0 = max subarray ending here with 0 deletions, dp1 = with 1 deletion
B) Use Kadane's variant with skip flag
C) Both A and B work
D) Prefix and suffix max arrays
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: DP: dp0[i] = max(dp0[i-1] + nums[i], nums[i]), dp1[i] = max(dp1[i-1] + nums[i], dp0[i-1])

questions.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II (unlimited transactions)
questions.problem_statement: Maximize profit with unlimited transactions but must sell before buying again.
options: A) Sum all positive day-to-day differences
B) Find local minima and maxima and sum differences
C) Both A and B work
D) DP with state holding/not holding stock
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Greedy: profit += max(0, prices[i] - prices[i-1]) captures all upward movements.

questions.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock III (at most 2 transactions)
questions.problem_statement: Maximize profit with at most two transactions.
options: A) DP with states: t1_cost, t1_profit, t2_cost, t2_profit
B) Split array into two halves and use best time I on each
C) Both A and B work
D) Use segment tree
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Left-to-right pass computes max profit until i; right-to-left pass computes max profit after i; combine.

questions.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock IV (at most k transactions)
questions.problem_statement: Maximize profit with at most k transactions.
options: A) DP: dp[k][i] = max(dp[k][i-1], prices[i] + max(prev - prices[j]))
B) Optimized with local and global arrays O(kn)
C) Both A and B work with optimization
D) Greedy only works for unlimited
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work with optimization
questions.explanation: When k > n/2, treat as unlimited (transaction count bounded by n/2).

questions.title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
questions.problem_statement: Find length of longest strictly increasing subsequence.
options: A) DP O(n^2): LIS[i] = max(LIS[j] + 1) for j < i and nums[j] < nums[i]
B) Patience sorting with binary search O(n log n)
C) Both A and B work, B is optimal
D) Greedy fails
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, B is optimal
questions.explanation: Patience sorting maintains piles of cards; binary search finds placement for each number.

questions.title: Number of Longest Increasing Subsequence
questions.problem_statement: Count number of longest increasing subsequences.
options: A) DP storing length and count for each position
B) Segment tree for length-count pair
C) Both A and B work
D) Use LIS length then backtracking
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: DP: for each i, update length[i] and count[i] based on all j < i with nums[j] < nums[i].

questions.title: Russian Doll Envelopes
questions.problem_statement: Maximum number of envelopes you can Russian doll (both width and height strictly increasing).
options: A) Sort by width ascending, height descending, then LIS on height
B) 2D DP O(n^2)
C) Both A and B work, A is optimal
D) Greedy fails
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, A is optimal
questions.explanation: Sorting by width and using LIS on height (with binary search) gives O(n log n).

questions.title: Maximum Length of Pair Chain
questions.problem_statement: Find longest chain of pairs where second < first of next pair.
options: A) Sort by first element, LIS-like DP O(n^2)
B) Greedy: sort by second element, greedy chain
C) Both A and B work, greedy O(n log n)
D) Interval scheduling variant
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, greedy O(n log n)
questions.explanation: Greedy by second element yields maximum chain length (similar to non-overlapping intervals).

questions.title: Coin Change II (number of combinations)
questions.problem_statement: Given coins, return number of combinations that sum to amount (order doesn't matter).
options: A) Outer loop coins, inner loop amount: dp[a] += dp[a-coin]
B) Outer loop amount, inner loop coins gives permutations
C) Both A and B but A gives combinations, B gives permutations
D) Both produce combinations if carefully ordered
questions.correct_answer: A) Outer loop coins, inner loop amount: dp[a] += dp[a-coin]
questions.explanation: Coin-outer order ensures each combination counted once (prevents permutations).

questions.title: Target Sum
questions.problem_statement: Assign + or - to each number to reach target sum.
options: A) Subset sum: sum(P) - sum(N) = target => sum(P) = (total + target)/2
B) DP with offset for negative sums
C) Both A and B work
D) Backtracking O(2^n)
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Transform to subset sum problem: count subsets with sum S = (total+target)/2.

questions.title: Ones and Zeroes
questions.problem_statement: Given binary strings, find max number you can form with m zeros and n ones.
options: A) 2D DP: dp[z][o] = max(dp[z][o], dp[z-zeros][o-ones] + 1)
B) 3D DP: dp[i][z][o] (i-th string)
C) Both A and B, A optimized
D) Greedy by ratio
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B, A optimized
questions.explanation: 2D DP (reverse order on zero/one loops) is 2D knapsack variant.

questions.title: Minimum Cost For Tickets
questions.problem_statement: Given days array and costs for 1-day, 7-day, 30-day passes, find minimum cost to cover all travel days.
options: A) DP for each day from 1 to 365: dp[i] = min(dp[i-1]+cost[0], dp[i-7]+cost[1], dp[i-30]+cost[2])
B) Use queue for 7-day and 30-day rolling minimum
C) Both A and B work
D) Greedy fails
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: DP with window or queue for rolling costs O(D) where D=last day.

questions.title: Knight Probability in Chessboard
questions.problem_statement: Probability that knight stays on board after k moves.
options: A) DP with probability distribution for each position
B) BFS with probability tracking
C) Both A and B work
D) Monte Carlo simulation
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: DP[k][i][j] = sum of dp[k-1][ni][nj]/8 for all valid knight moves.

questions.title: Super Egg Drop
questions.problem_statement: Given k eggs and n floors, find minimum moves to determine critical floor.
options: A) DP: dp[k][n] = min(max(dp[k-1][x-1], dp[k][n-x]) + 1) over x
B) Math approach using binomial coefficients
C) Both A and B work, B is O(k log n)
D) Binary search with eggs
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, B is O(k log n)
questions.explanation: DP O(kn^2) can be optimized; math approach finds smallest m such that sum(C(m,1..k)) ≥ n.

questions.title: Burst Balloons
questions.problem_statement: Burst balloons to maximize coins; coins = nums[left]*nums[i]*nums[right].
options: A) DP on interval [i][j] assuming boundary balloons are last burst except i-1 and j+1
B) Add 1s at boundaries, DP with divide and conquer
C) Both A and B
D) Greedy pop smallest balloon
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B
questions.explanation: Interval DP O(n^3): dp[i][j] = max(dp[i][k-1] + nums[i-1]*nums[k]*nums[j+1] + dp[k+1][j]).

questions.title: Delete and Earn
questions.problem_statement: Delete value, earn its points, but cannot delete value+1 or value-1.
options: A) Transform to House Robber: points[i] = sum of all nums equal to i
B) DP: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + points[i])
C) Both A and B work
D) Sort and greedy
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Count frequencies, apply house robber DP on value array (1 to max).

questions.title: Minimum Falling Path Sum
questions.problem_statement: Find minimum sum of falling path from top to bottom in n x n matrix.
options: A) DP: dp[i][j] = matrix[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j-1], dp[i-1][j], dp[i-1][j+1])
B) BFS with priority queue
C) Both A and B, DP simpler
D) Greedy per row fails
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B, DP simpler
questions.explanation: Bottom-up DP O(n^2) updates each cell from previous row.

questions.title: Triangle (Path Sum)
questions.problem_statement: Given triangle array, find minimum path sum from top to bottom.
options: A) DP bottom-up: dp[j] = triangle[i][j] + min(dp[j], dp[j+1])
B) Top-down recursion with memoization
C) Both A and B work, A O(n) space
