D) Dijkstra on DAG
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, A O(n) space
questions.explanation: Bottom-up in-place DP updates row from previous row, O(1) extra space.

questions.title: Paint House (LeetCode Premium - concept question)
questions.problem_statement: Paint row of houses with 3 colors, no two adjacent same color, minimize cost.
options: A) DP: dp[i][c] = cost[i][c] + min(dp[i-1][other two colors])
B) Track two minimums from previous row for O(1) space
C) Both A and B work
D) Greedy per house fails
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Standard DP O(n*3) with state of previous house's color, can be optimized by tracking top two min costs.

questions.title: Paint Fence (LeetCode Premium - concept question)
questions.problem_statement: Paint n posts with k colors, no more than 2 adjacent same color.
options: A) DP: same[i] = diff[i-1]; diff[i] = (k-1)*(same[i-1] + diff[i-1])
B) Total = same[i] + diff[i]
C) Both A and B
D) Combinatorics formula
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B
questions.explanation: same[i]=posts ending with same color as previous; diff[i]=different from previous.

questions.title: Number of Longest Increasing Subsequence
questions.problem_statement: Return number of longest increasing subsequences.
options: A) DP storing length and count for each index
B) Segment tree for range max length and count
C) Both A and B work
D) LIS length then counting paths
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: DP O(n^2) double loop; segment tree optimizes O(n log n).

questions.title: Integer Break
questions.problem_statement: Break integer into sum of positive integers to maximize product.
options: A) DP: dp[i] = max(max(j, dp[j]) * max(i-j, dp[i-j]))
B) Math: break into 3s if n>4, else n itself
C) Both A and B work
D) Greedy use 3s
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Optimal factor is 3 (except for 4 = 2*2 > 3*1).

questions.title: Count Numbers with Unique Digits
questions.problem_statement: Count numbers with unique digits in range [0, 10^n).
options: A) DP: f(k) = 9 * 9 * 8 * ... for k digits
B) Backtracking count permutations
C) Both A and B, formula is O(n)
D) Iterate and check
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B, formula is O(n)
questions.explanation: Formula: for k=1:10, k=2:9*9, k=3:9*9*8, ... sum for 0..n digits.

questions.title: Predict the Winner
questions.problem_statement: Two players pick from ends of array, player with higher sum wins. Can player1 win?
options: A) DP: dp[i][j] = max(nums[i] - dp[i+1][j], nums[j] - dp[i][j-1])
B) Recursion with memoization
C) Both A and B work
D) Greedy pick larger end fails
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: DP state represents max score difference current player can achieve from subarray i..j.

questions.title: Stone Game
questions.problem_statement: Two players pick stones from ends; even length pile; player1 always wins?
options: A) DP as predict the winner
B) Math: player1 can always win by choosing odd/even parity
C) Both A and B, B proves always true
D) Return true always
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B, B proves always true
questions.explanation: Sum of even indices vs odd indices; player1 forces picking all evens or odds.

metadata.note: Second set of 160 unique questions. No overlap with Set 1. Covers arrays, strings, DP, graphs, trees, greedy, bit manipulation, design, and math.

questions.title: Find First and Last Position of Element in Sorted Array
questions.problem_statement: Given sorted array nums and target, find starting and ending position of target. If not found, return [-1, -1].
options: A) Linear scan from left and right O(n)
B) Two binary searches: one for left bound, one for right bound
C) Single binary search then expand left/right
D) Both B and C are valid approaches
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C are valid approaches
questions.explanation: Two separate binary searches for leftmost and rightmost indices give O(log n). Single search with expansion degrades to O(n) in worst case.

questions.title: Search Insert Position
questions.problem_statement: Given sorted array and target, return index if found, else index where it would be inserted.
options: A) Iterate from left O(n)
B) Binary search returning left pointer
C) Use Python's bisect library
D) Both B and C work
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C work
questions.explanation: Standard binary search: when low > high, low is the insertion position.

questions.title: Find Peak Element
questions.problem_statement: Find peak element (greater than neighbors) in array. Multiple peaks, return any.
options: A) Linear scan O(n)
B) Binary search comparing mid with mid+1
C) Recursive divide and conquer
D) Both B and C achieve O(log n)
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C achieve O(log n)
questions.explanation: Binary search: if nums[mid] < nums[mid+1], peak is on right; else left.

questions.title: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
questions.problem_statement: Find minimum element in rotated sorted array (no duplicates).
options: A) Linear scan O(n)
B) Binary search comparing mid with rightmost
C) Find pivot via binary search and return next element
D) Both B and C work
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C work
questions.explanation: Binary search: if nums[mid] > nums[right], min in right half; else min in left half (including mid).

questions.title: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array II (with duplicates)
questions.problem_statement: Find minimum in rotated sorted array with duplicates.
options: A) Linear scan O(n) works
B) Binary search with extra step: if nums[mid] == nums[right], decrement right
C) Both A and B but B is average O(log n), worst O(n)
D) Cannot use binary search at all
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B but B is average O(log n), worst O(n)
questions.explanation: When nums[mid] == nums[right], reduce search space by one (right--).

questions.title: Median of Two Sorted Arrays
questions.problem_statement: Find median of two sorted arrays in O(log(m+n)).
options: A) Merge arrays and find median O(m+n)
B) Binary search on smaller array partitioning
C) Use heap of size (m+n)/2+1
D) Both B and C but B is O(log min(m,n))
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C but B is O(log min(m,n))
questions.explanation: Partition both arrays such that left half elements <= right half, adjusting partition index via binary search.

questions.title: Kth Smallest Element in a Sorted Matrix
questions.problem_statement: Find kth smallest element in row-wise and column-wise sorted matrix.
options: A) Flatten and sort O(n^2 log n)
B) Binary search on value range with count <= mid
C) Min-heap merging rows
D) Both B and C work, B is O(n log range)
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C work, B is O(n log range)
questions.explanation: Binary search between matrix[0][0] and matrix[n-1][n-1]; count number of elements <= mid using row-wise binary search.

questions.title: Kth Largest Element in an Array
questions.problem_statement: Find kth largest element in unsorted array.
options: A) Sort and pick O(n log n)
B) Min-heap of size k O(n log k)
C) Quick Select (Hoare's selection) average O(n)
D) All of the above
questions.correct_answer: D) All of the above
questions.explanation: Quick select has average O(n) but worst O(n^2). Heap gives O(n log k) guarantees.

questions.title: Find All Duplicates in an Array
questions.problem_statement: Given array of integers 1 ≤ a[i] ≤ n, some appear twice, find all duplicates.
options: A) Use hash set O(n) space
B) Mark seen by negating value at index nums[i]-1
C) Sort and scan O(n log n)
D) Both B and B achieve O(n) time, B uses O(1) space
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and B achieve O(n) time, B uses O(1) space
questions.explanation: When visiting nums[i], if value at abs(nums[i])-1 is negative, it's duplicate; else negate.

questions.title: Find the Celebrity (LeetCode Premium - concept question)
questions.problem_statement: Find celebrity (knows nobody, everyone knows them) using O(n) API calls.
options: A) Brute force O(n^2) API calls
B) Two-pointer: eliminate one candidate at a time, then verify
C) Stack elimination method
D) Both B and C achieve O(n)
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C achieve O(n)
questions.explanation: Elimination pass: if a knows b, a cannot be celebrity; else b cannot. Then verify candidate.

questions.title: Increasing Triplet Subsequence
questions.problem_statement: Given array, return true if exists increasing triplet (i < j < k with nums[i] < nums[j] < nums[k]).
options: A) O(n^3) brute force
B) O(n) greedy tracking first and second minimum
C) O(n log n) using LIS length
D) Both B and C work
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C work
questions.explanation: Greedy: keep first = INF, second = INF; update first or second; when current > second, return true.

questions.title: Maximum Average Subarray I
questions.problem_statement: Find maximum average of contiguous subarray of length k.
options: A) For each window, compute sum O(n*k)
B) Sliding window: maintain sum, subtract left, add right
C) Prefix sum array O(n)
D) Both B and C achieve O(n)
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C achieve O(n)
questions.explanation: Sliding window updates sum in O(1) per step, tracking maximum average.

questions.title: Maximum Sum Circular Subarray
questions.problem_statement: Find maximum subarray sum in circular array (wrap-around allowed).
options: A) Apply Kadane twice: normal and circular = total - min subarray sum
B) Transform array: double and apply Kadane with length ≤ n
C) Both A and B work, A is O(n)
D) Brute force O(n^2)
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, A is O(n)
questions.explanation: Maximum circular = max( normal max, total sum - min subarray sum ). Edge case: all negative.

questions.title: Subarray Product Less Than K
questions.problem_statement: Count number of contiguous subarrays where product of elements < k.
options: A) Generate all subarrays O(n^2)
B) Sliding window: expand right, shrink left when product >= k, add window size to count
C) Prefix product with binary search for logs
D) Both B and C work, B is O(n)
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C work, B is O(n)
questions.explanation: For each right, find largest left such that product < k; number of subarrays ending at right = right-left+1.

questions.title: Fruit Into Baskets
questions.problem_statement: Maximum number of fruits to collect with 2 baskets (at most 2 types).
options: A) Sliding window with hash map tracking fruit counts
B) When third type appears, shrink window until only 2 types
C) Both A and B describe same approach
D) Greedy pick longest sequence
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B describe same approach
questions.explanation: Standard longest substring with at most 2 distinct characters (fruits).

questions.title: Longest Repeating Character Replacement
questions.problem_statement: Replace at most k characters to make longest repeating substring.
options: A) Expand window, maintain max frequency, shrink if window size - maxFreq > k
B) Binary search on answer O(n log n)
C) Both A and B work, A is O(n)
D) Greedy replace most frequent char
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, A is O(n)
questions.explanation: Sliding window ensures condition (window length - max count) ≤ k; expand right, shrink left when violated.

questions.title: Find All Anagrams in a String
questions.problem_statement: Find all start indices of p's anagrams in s.
options: A) For each sliding window, compare sorted string O(n*m log m)
B) Frequency array sliding window
C) Hash map with character counts and required matches counter
D) Both B and C give O(n) solution
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C give O(n) solution
questions.explanation: Sliding window of length len(p), maintain frequency counts and increment match count when counts match exactly.

questions.title: Permutation in String
questions.problem_statement: Check if s1's permutation is substring of s2.
options: A) Generate all permutations of s1 and check substr O(n!)
B) Sliding window with frequency array same as find all anagrams (return any match)
C) Sort and compare windows O(n log n)
D) Both B and frequency approach work
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and frequency approach work
questions.explanation: Same as finding anagrams; window of length len(s1) and compare frequency arrays.

questions.title: Rotate Array
questions.problem_statement: Rotate array to the right by k steps in-place.
options: A) Use extra array of same size O(n) space
B) Reverse entire array, reverse first k, reverse last n-k
C) Cyclic replacements O(n)
D) Both B and C work, B is simpler
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and C work, B is simpler
questions.explanation: Three-reverse approach: reverse(0,n-1); reverse(0,k-1); reverse(k,n-1).

questions.title: Shortest Unsorted Continuous Subarray
questions.problem_statement: Find length of smallest subarray that when sorted makes whole array sorted.
options: A) Sort array and compare O(n log n)
B) Find left boundary where nums[i] > min of right, right boundary where nums[i] < max of left
C) Use stack for next smaller/greater
D) Both B and O(n) approach work
questions.correct_answer: D) Both B and O(n) approach work
questions.explanation: Scan left to right for last index where nums[i] < maxSoFar; scan right to left for first index where nums[i] > minSoFar.

questions.title: Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array II (at most twice)
questions.problem_statement: Remove duplicates such that each appears at most twice, return new length.
options: A) Use hash map count, rebuild O(n) space
B) Two pointers: i for writing, j for scanning, track count
C) Both A and B work, B is in-place
D) Sort not needed as already sorted
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, B is in-place
questions.explanation: Keep count of current element; write if count ≤ 2, else skip.

questions.title: Beautiful Arrangement
questions.problem_statement: Count permutations of 1..n where for each i, i divisible by perm[i] or perm[i] divisible by i.
options: A) Backtracking with pruning O(k) per arrangement
B) DP with bitmask: dp[mask] = number of ways
C) Both A and B work, DP O(n*2^n)
D) Greedy assignment works
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, DP O(n*2^n)
questions.explanation: Bitmask DP: state is used numbers, try placing next number at position pos+1.

questions.title: Beautiful Arrangement II
questions.problem_statement: Construct array of 1..n with exactly k distinct absolute differences between consecutive elements.
options: A) Random shuffle and check O(n!)
B) Greedy: place 1, n, 2, n-1, 3, n-2,... to get max differences, then fill remaining monotonically
C) Both constructive method
D) Use two pointers for k-1 distinct differences then natural order
questions.correct_answer: D) Use two pointers for k-1 distinct differences then natural order
questions.explanation: First k+1 elements: 1, n, 2, n-1, ... produce decreasing differences; remaining fill normally.

questions.title: Gray Code
questions.problem_statement: Generate Gray code sequence of length 2^n.
options: A) Recursive: generate for n-1, reflect and add 2^(n-1)
B) Binary to Gray: i ^ (i>>1)
C) Both A and B produce valid sequence
D) BFS on hypercube
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B produce valid sequence
questions.explanation: Formula Gray(n) = n ^ (n>>1) generates i-th Gray code in O(1).

questions.title: Largest Number
questions.problem_statement: Arrange numbers to form largest number (e.g., [3,30,34,5,9] -> '9534330').
options: A) Sort by string comparison: (a+b) > (b+a)
B) Convert to string, sort descending as strings, handle leading zeros
C) Both A and B work
D) Radix sort on digits
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Custom comparator (x+y vs y+x) ensures largest concatenation.

questions.title: Largest Divisible Subset
questions.problem_statement: Find largest subset where every pair (a,b) satisfies a%b==0 or b%a==0.
options: A) Sort array, DP storing size and previous index
B) After sorting, for each i, try all j < i
C) Both A and B describe same O(n^2) DP
D) Greedy pick smallest factors
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B describe same O(n^2) DP
questions.explanation: Sort array, dp[i] = max(dp[j] + 1) for nums[i] % nums[j] == 0; track path.

questions.title: Delete Nodes And Return Forest
questions.problem_statement: Given binary tree and nodes to delete, return forest (roots of remaining trees).
options: A) DFS post-order: if node to delete, add children as new roots
B) BFS level tracking, remove nodes
C) Both A and B but A is standard
D) Iterative with stack
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B but A is standard
questions.explanation: Post-order recursion: if node in to_delete, return null and add non-null children to result.

questions.title: All Nodes Distance K in Binary Tree
questions.problem_statement: Find all nodes at distance exactly k from target node.
options: A) Build graph from tree then BFS O(n)
B) DFS to find target path then DFS in subtrees
C) Both A and B work, graph approach simpler
D) Use parent pointers and BFS
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, graph approach simpler
questions.explanation: Convert to undirected graph via parent references, then BFS from target for k distance.

questions.title: Flip Binary Tree To Match Preorder Traversal
questions.problem_statement: Given binary tree and voyage (preorder), flip nodes (swap children) to match voyage. Return flipped nodes or -1.
options: A) DFS with index pointer: if root.val != voyage[i], return -1; else if left child exists and left.val != voyage[i+1], flip
B) BFS compare levels
C) Both A and DFS solution
D) Dynamic programming on subtrees
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and DFS solution
questions.explanation: DFS: if left child value != next in preorder, swap left/right; increment index and recurse.

questions.title: Binary Tree Cameras
questions.problem_statement: Place cameras on nodes such that each node is covered (by itself or neighbor). Minimum cameras.
options: A) Greedy post-order: states 0=uncovered,1=covered,2=has camera
B) DP on tree with states: camera, covered by parent, covered by child
C) Both A and B, greedy O(n)
D) BFS level coverage fails
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B, greedy O(n)
questions.explanation: DFS returns state; if any child is uncovered, place camera at current node.

questions.title: Recover Binary Search Tree
questions.problem_statement: Two elements of BST swapped, recover tree without changing structure.
options: A) In-order traversal into array, find two swapped, fix O(n) space
B) Morris traversal (threaded tree) O(1) space
C) Recursive in-order tracking prev and first/mid/last
D) Both A and C work, C is O(h) space
questions.correct_answer: D) Both A and C work, C is O(h) space
questions.explanation: In-order DFS: detect first violation (prev > curr) and second violation, swap values.

questions.title: Path Sum III (count paths equal to target)
questions.problem_statement: Count paths with sum equal to target, path can start/end anywhere but must go downwards.
options: A) For each node, DFS to find all paths from node O(n^2)
B) Prefix sum with hash map (similar to subarray sum equals k) on DFS
C) Both A and B work, B is O(n)
D) Greedy sum fails
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, B is O(n)
questions.explanation: DFS with hash map storing cumulative sum frequencies; at each node, add count of (currSum - target).

questions.title: Flatten Binary Tree to Linked List
questions.problem_statement: Flatten binary tree to linked list in preorder using right pointers.
options: A) Recursive: flatten left, flatten right, attach right after left
B) Iterative using stack O(n)
C) Morris traversal: find predecessor, link
D) All of the above
questions.correct_answer: D) All of the above
questions.explanation: Recursive O(n): flatten left, move left to right, attach original right to end of new right.

questions.title: Populating Next Right Pointers in Each Node
questions.problem_statement: Connect each node to its next right node (perfect binary tree).
options: A) BFS level order using queue O(n) space
B) Recursive using parent's next pointer O(1) space
C) Both A and B, B is constant space
D) Use stack for DFS
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B, B is constant space
questions.explanation: Start from root, connect left child's next to right child's, and right child's next to parent's next's left.

questions.title: Path Sum II (find all paths equal to target)
questions.problem_statement: Return all root-to-leaf paths where sum equals target.
options: A) DFS with path tracking, when leaf and sum==target, add copy of path
B) BFS storing path along with node
C) Both A and B, A is classic
D) Backtracking with pruning
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B, A is classic
questions.explanation: Pre-order DFS, maintain current path and remaining sum, backtrack after recursion.

questions.title: Sum Root to Leaf Numbers
questions.problem_statement: Each root-to-leaf path represents number, return sum of all numbers.
options: A) DFS with current number = prev*10 + node.val
B) BFS with queue storing node and running number
C) Both A and B work
D) Convert each path to string then int
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: DFS accumulates numbers and sums when leaf reached.

questions.title: Binary Tree Right Side View
questions.problem_statement: Return values of nodes visible from right side (last node each level).
options: A) BFS level order, add last node of each level
B) DFS with depth: first time at depth add node (if right first then left)
C) Both A and B work
D) Reverse BFS from right
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: DFS: pre-order with right child first, if level == result size, add node.

questions.title: Kth Smallest Element in a BST
questions.problem_statement: Find kth smallest element in BST (1-indexed).
options: A) In-order traversal, store list pick k-1 O(n)
B) In-order traversal with early stop at k
C) Augmented BST with left size O(log n) for rank
D) Both A and B work, B O(k) time
questions.correct_answer: D) Both A and B work, B O(k) time
questions.explanation: In-order DFS increments counter when visiting node; when counter == k, record answer.

questions.title: Convert BST to Greater Tree
questions.problem_statement: Convert BST to Greater Tree where each node's value = sum of all greater nodes + its original value.
options: A) Reverse in-order (right-root-left) accumulating sum and updating node
B) Two passes: collect values, compute prefix sums, update
C) Both A and B work, A is O(n) in-place
D) Use stack for iterative reverse in-order
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, A is O(n) in-place
questions.explanation: Reverse in-order traversal maintains cumulative sum and updates current node value.

questions.title: Find Mode in Binary Search Tree
questions.problem_statement: Find most frequent element(s) in BST (duplicates allowed).
options: A) In-order traversal into list, count frequencies O(n) space
B) Morris traversal with constant space, track current count and max count
C) Both A and B work
D) Use hash map in any traversal
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: In-order traversal yields sorted sequence; count consecutive duplicates, update mode list.

questions.title: Minimum Absolute Difference in BST
questions.problem_statement: Find minimum absolute difference between any two nodes in BST.
options: A) In-order traversal, compute difference between consecutive values
B) Use set of all values, O(n^2) pairs
C) Both A and B but A is O(n)
D) BFS and compare neighbors
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B but A is O(n)
questions.explanation: In-order traversal gives sorted order; min diff occurs between consecutive elements.

questions.title: Range Sum of BST
questions.problem_statement: Given BST and range [low, high], return sum of node values within range.
options: A) Recursive DFS: if node.val < low, go right; if node.val > high, go left; else add and recurse both
B) BFS queue with pruning
C) Both A and B work
D) Flatten and sum filter
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: DFS pruning uses BST property to skip entire subtrees.

questions.title: Trim a Binary Search Tree
questions.problem_statement: Trim BST such that all node values lie within [L,R] (keep shape).
options: A) Recursion: if node.val < L, return trim(right); if node.val > R, return trim(left); else node.left=trim(left), node.right=trim(right), return node
B) Iterative using stack to remove nodes out of range
C) Both A and B work, A is standard
D) Convert to sorted list and rebuild
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, A is standard
questions.explanation: Recursive approach leveraging BST property to discard subtrees entirely out of range.

questions.title: Insert into a Binary Search Tree
questions.problem_statement: Insert a value into BST (return root).
options: A) Recursive: if root null, create node; else go left or right accordingly
B) Iterative while loop with parent tracking
C) Both A and B work
D) Add as leaf, then rebalance
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Standard insertion: compare value, traverse until null, insert as leaf.

questions.title: Delete Node in a BST
questions.problem_statement: Delete a node with given key from BST.
options: A) Find node, cases: leaf, one child, two children (replace with inorder successor/predecessor)
B) Recursive approach handling each case
C) Both A and B represent same method
D) Rebuild tree without key
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B represent same method
questions.explanation: Two-children case: replace value with inorder successor's value, then delete successor.

questions.title: Serialize and Deserialize BST
questions.problem_statement: Design algorithm to serialize/deserialize BST (more efficient than BT).
options: A) Preorder traversal with null markers (same as BT)
B) Use min/max bounds to reconstruct without null markers
C) Both A and B work, B is more compact
D) Level order with sentinel
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, B is more compact
questions.explanation: Preorder encoding, reconstruct using BST property and range (low, high) to place nodes.

questions.title: Unique Binary Search Trees (count)
questions.problem_statement: Count number of structurally unique BSTs with n nodes labeled 1..n.
options: A) DP: dp[n] = sum(dp[i-1]*dp[n-i]) for i=1..n (Catalan number)
B) Direct Catalan: C(2n,n)/(n+1)
C) Both A and B give same result
D) Recursion with memoization
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B give same result
questions.explanation: DP recurrence computes Catalan numbers in O(n^2) or O(n) with binomial coefficient.

questions.title: Unique Binary Search Trees II (generate all)
questions.problem_statement: Generate all structurally unique BSTs with n nodes.
options: A) Recursion: for each i as root, generate left trees from 1..i-1, right from i+1..n, combine
B) DP storing list of trees for each range
C) Both A and B are same
D) Backtracking with pruning
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B are same
questions.explanation: Divide and conquer: pick root, recursively build left and right subtrees, Cartesian product.

questions.title: Different Ways to Add Parentheses
questions.problem_statement: Given expression with +,-,*, return all possible results from different parenthesizations.
options: A) Divide at each operator, recursively compute left and right results, combine
B) DP on substrings
C) Both A and B, A is natural
D) Lexicographic generation of parentheses
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B, A is natural
questions.explanation: Divide and conquer: split at operator, compute all results from left and right, then combine via operator.

questions.title: Partition to K Equal Sum Subsets
questions.problem_statement: Given array and k, check if can partition into k subsets with equal sum.
options: A) Backtracking: assign each number to one of k subsets, prune with sum
B) DP with bitmask: dp[mask] = sum % target
C) Both A and B work, bitmask DP O(n*2^n)
D) Greedy largest first sometimes fails
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, bitmask DP O(n*2^n)
questions.explanation: Backtracking with sorting descending and pruning when subset sum > target reduces search.

questions.title: Matchsticks to Square
questions.problem_statement: Given matchsticks, can they form a square? (similar to partition to 4 equal sum subsets)
options: A) Partition into 4 equal sum subsets using backtracking
B) Same as partition to k=4, with side length = total/4
C) Both A and B
D) Greedy fails
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B
questions.explanation: Backtracking assigning sticks to four sides, with pruning when side sum exceeds target.

questions.title: Can I Win
questions.problem_statement: Two players pick numbers 1..maxChoosableInteger. First to reach total >= desiredTotal wins. Can first player win?
options: A) Min-max recursion with memoization on bitmask of used numbers
B) DP boolean[] representing used set, compute winning states
C) Both A and B
D) Math: if desiredTotal <= maxChoosableInteger, true
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B
questions.explanation: State is used numbers mask; current player wins if any move leads to opponent losing.

questions.title: Flatten Nested List Iterator
questions.problem_statement: Design iterator to flatten nested list of integers.
options: A) Recursively flatten entire list into array on constructor O(n)
B) Stack of iterators: hasNext() may advance, next() returns current integer
C) Both A and B work, B is lazy
D) Use queue
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, B is lazy
questions.explanation: Stack of pairs (list, index) or iterators; hasNext() pushes until integer found.

questions.title: Design Twitter
questions.problem_statement: Design Twitter with postTweet, getNewsFeed, follow, unfollow.
options: A) Store tweets in list per user, feed = merge k lists with heap O(10*log k)
B) Use global tweet list with timestamps, filter followees
C) Both A and B, A is common
D) Use database style
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B, A is common
questions.explanation: For each user, store own tweets. Feed: get recent tweets from user and followed users, use max-heap by time.

questions.title: Design HashMap
questions.problem_statement: Design hash map without built-in libs (put, get, remove).
options: A) Use array of buckets (linked list for collision) size 10^4
B) Use linear probing open addressing
C) Both A and B valid
D) Use binary search tree per bucket
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B valid
questions.explanation: Chaining with linked list or array of nodes is most common.

questions.title: Design HashSet
questions.problem_statement: Design hash set without built-in libraries.
options: A) Boolean array of size 10^6 (space heavy)
B) Array of buckets with linked list
C) Both A and B work, B is scalable
D) Use tree set
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, B is scalable
questions.explanation: If key range known and moderate, boolean array works; otherwise chaining.

questions.title: LRU Cache
questions.problem_statement: Least Recently Used cache implementation with O(1) get/put.
options: A) HashMap + doubly linked list (ordered by use)
B) LinkedHashMap (Java) / OrderedDict (Python)
C) Both A and B achieve O(1)
D) Use two stacks for recency
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B achieve O(1)
questions.explanation: Custom doubly linked list with map for node lookup; built-in structures also work.

questions.title: LFU Cache
questions.problem_statement: Least Frequently Used cache with O(1) average operations.
options: A) HashMap key->node, frequency map -> doubly linked list of nodes
B) Use priority queue with frequency andtimestamp (O(log n))
C) Both A and B, A is optimized
D) Single linked list with frequency sweep
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B, A is optimized
questions.explanation: Use two maps: key to node (value,freq); freq to linked list of keys; maintain minFreq.

questions.title: Design Circular Deque
questions.problem_statement: Design circular double-ended queue with fixed size.
options: A) Use array with front and rear pointers
B) Use doubly linked list
C) Both A and B work, A is space-efficient
D) Use two stacks
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, A is space-efficient
questions.explanation: Circular array with modulo indexing: front and rear pointers, handle full/empty conditions.

questions.title: Design Circular Queue
questions.problem_statement: Design circular queue with enQueue, deQueue, Front, Rear, isFull, isEmpty.
options: A) Use array with head and tail pointers, size counter
B) Use linked list
C) Both A and B, array is typical
D) Use two stacks
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B, array is typical
questions.explanation: Circular buffer: keep one slot empty to distinguish full vs empty (or maintain count).

questions.title: Encode and Decode TinyURL
questions.problem_statement: Design URL shortening service.
options: A) Incremental ID -> base62 encoding
B) Random fixed-length string with collision check
C) Both A and B common approaches
D) Use hash of URL
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B common approaches
questions.explanation: Store mapping in DB or map; encode ID to short key (base62); or generate random key.

questions.title: Shuffle an Array
questions.problem_statement: Shuffle array in-place with equal probability.
options: A) Generate random index and swap with current index (Fisher-Yates)
B) Use sort with random comparator (biased)
C) Both A and B but A is uniform
D) Use random permutation library
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B but A is uniform
questions.explanation: Fisher-Yates: for i from n-1 to 1, swap with random index 0..i.

questions.title: Random Pick Index (Reservoir Sampling)
questions.problem_statement: Pick random index of target in array (multiple occurrences) with equal probability.
options: A) Collect all indices and pick random O(n) not streaming
B) Reservoir sampling: count occurrences, pick with probability 1/count
C) Both A and B, B works for streaming
D) Preprocess index list
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B, B works for streaming
questions.explanation: Reservoir: keep result, count=1; for next matching index, replace result with probability 1/count.

questions.title: Insert Delete GetRandom O(1)
questions.problem_statement: Data structure supporting insert, delete, getRandom in O(1) average.
options: A) HashMap + ArrayList: map element->index, list stores elements
B) Two HashMaps (value->index and index->value) for double validation
C) Both A and B, A is standard
D) Use tree and array
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B, A is standard
questions.explanation: Delete: swap with last element, update map, pop from list.

questions.title: Design Underground System
questions.problem_statement: Track check-in/check-out with average travel time between stations.
options: A) Two maps: checkIns (id -> (station, time)), averages (start+end -> (sum, count))
B) One map storing trips, compute on the fly
C) Both A and B, A is efficient
D) Use database
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B, A is efficient
questions.explanation: On check-in, store in map; on check-out, lookup check-in, update aggregates for route.

questions.title: Time Based Key-Value Store
questions.problem_statement: Store multiple values per key with timestamps, get value at given timestamp.
options: A) HashMap mapping key to list of (timestamp, value) pairs, binary search on timestamp list
B) TreeMap per key for floorKey
C) Both A and B work
D) Use array of all timestamps
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Sorted list per key; get: binary search to find largest timestamp ≤ given stamp.

questions.title: Snapshot Array
questions.problem_statement: Array that supports snap() and get(index, snap_id).
options: A) For each index, list of (snap_id, value), binary search on snap_id
B) Store full array per snapshot (space heavy)
C) Both A and B, A is space-efficient
D) Use difference array
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B, A is space-efficient
questions.explanation: Each index maintains a tree map or list; when set, add (snap_id, value) -> later get: lower bound.

questions.title: Number of Matching Subsequences
questions.problem_statement: Count strings in array that are subsequences of s.
options: A) For each word, check subsequence O(s.length * words.length)
B) Preprocess s: array of lists for each char (indices), binary search for each word
C) Both A and B, B is faster
D) Build suffix automaton
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B, B is faster
questions.explanation: Store for each character the list of positions; for each word, binary search to find next occurrence.

questions.title: Short Encoding of Words
questions.problem_statement: Find minimal length of reference string that encodes list of words (each word ends with #).
options: A) Insert reversed words into trie, answer = sum(node depth +1) for leaf nodes
B) Sort by length descending, remove words that are suffix of previous
C) Both A and B work
D) Use hash set of suffixes
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Trie of reversed words: leaf nodes represent words not suffix of another, sum lengths + #.

questions.title: Replace Words
questions.problem_statement: Replace all words in sentence with their shortest root from dictionary.
options: A) Trie of roots, for each word in sentence, find shortest prefix match
B) For each word, check all roots O(n*m)
C) Both A and B, A is faster
D) Sort roots and binary search
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B, A is faster
questions.explanation: Build trie of roots; for each word, traverse trie until end (if prefix found) else keep original.

questions.title: Map Sum Pairs
questions.problem_statement: Insert key-value pairs, sum of values for keys starting with given prefix.
options: A) Trie with node storing sum of all values in subtree
B) HashMap storing key->value, for prefix sum iterate over keys O(n)
C) Both A and B, A is O(L) per operation
D) Use prefix hash map
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B, A is O(L) per operation
questions.explanation: Trie: each node keeps cumulative sum; on insert, update all nodes in path.

questions.title: Implement Stack using Queues
questions.problem_statement: Implement stack using only two queues.
options: A) Make push O(1), pop O(n) by rotating queue
B) Make push O(n) by moving elements to second queue, pop O(1)
C) Both A and B are valid
D) Use single queue with rotation
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B are valid
questions.explanation: Two approaches: costly push (move all to other queue then back) or costly pop (dequeue all but last).

questions.title: Number of Recent Calls
questions.problem_statement: Count number of pings in last 3000 milliseconds.
options: A) Use queue, push new ping, pop until t - front <= 3000, return size
B) Use list and binary search for left bound
C) Both A and B work, A is simple
D) Use sliding window
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, A is simple
questions.explanation: Maintain queue of timestamps; at each ping, enqueue and remove outdated from front.

questions.title: Moving Average from Data Stream (LeetCode Premium - concept)
questions.problem_statement: Calculate moving average of last size elements.
options: A) Use circular array with sum variable
B) Use queue, maintain running sum
C) Both A and B
D) Store all values and recalculate
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B
questions.explanation: Queue: enqueue, if size > window, dequeue; update sum; average = sum / current size.

questions.title: Find Largest Value in Each Tree Row
questions.problem_statement: Return largest value per level in binary tree.
options: A) BFS level order, track max per level
B) DFS with level parameter, update max per level
C) Both A and B work
D) Use priority queue per level
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: BFS: for each level compute max; DFS: pass level, maintain array maxAtLevel.

questions.title: Cousins in Binary Tree
questions.problem_statement: Two nodes are cousins if same depth but different parents.
options: A) BFS storing depth and parent for each node
B) DFS depth and parent search
C) Both A and B work
D) Use LCA
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Traverse tree, record depth and parent; compare after traversal.

questions.title: Merge Two Binary Trees
questions.problem_statement: Merge two trees by summing overlapping nodes.
options: A) Recursive: merge(t1.left, t2.left) and merge(t1.right, t2.right)
B) BFS using queue with pairs
C) Both A and B work
D) In-order traversal and merge
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Recursive: if both null return null; new node = (t1?val:0)+(t2?val:0); recurse children.

questions.title: Average of Levels in Binary Tree
questions.problem_statement: Return average of node values per level.
options: A) BFS level order, compute sum and count
B) DFS with level tracking
C) Both A and B work
D) Use two stacks
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: BFS: process each level separately; sum values, divide by level size.

questions.title: Leaf-Similar Trees
questions.problem_statement: Check if two trees have same leaf sequence from left to right.
options: A) DFS to collect leaf values into arrays, compare
B) DFS with generator and compare lazily
C) Both A and B work
D) BFS level order leaf detection
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Perform DFS on each tree, add leaf values to list; compare lists.

questions.title: Sum of Left Leaves
questions.problem_statement: Find sum of all left leaves in binary tree.
options: A) DFS with flag tracking if node is left child
B) BFS with node and isLeft flag
C) Both A and B work
D) Morris traversal
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Recursive: if left child and leaf, add value, else recurse left+right with isLeft flag.

questions.title: Find Bottom Left Tree Value
questions.problem_statement: Find leftmost value in bottom row of binary tree.
options: A) BFS level order, store first element of each level, return last level's first
B) DFS with depth, track maxDepth and value
C) Both A and B work
D) Reverse level order
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: BFS: process right to left, last node processed is bottom-left; or DFS with depth tracking.

questions.title: Minimum Depth of Binary Tree
questions.problem_statement: Find minimum depth (shortest root-to-leaf path).
options: A) BFS: first leaf reached gives min depth
B) DFS recursion: if leaf return 1, else 1+min(left,right) (but check null cases)
C) Both A and B work, BFS stops early
D) Use post-order
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, BFS stops early
questions.explanation: BFS returns as soon as leaf found; DFS checks both subtrees.

questions.title: Convert Sorted Array to Binary Search Tree
questions.problem_statement: Convert sorted array to height-balanced BST.
options: A) Pick middle element as root, recursively build left and right
B) Use two pointers and recursion
C) Both A and B describe same approach
D) Build tree by inserting sequentially (unbalanced)
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B describe same approach
questions.explanation: Median as root ensures balance; O(n) time, O(log n) recursion stack.

questions.title: Two Sum IV - Input is a BST
questions.problem_statement: Find if there exist two nodes summing to k in BST.
options: A) In-order traversal to sorted list, two-pointer sum
B) BFS with hash set, for each node check if (k - node.val) exists
C) Both A and B work
D) Use recursion with set
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: In-order gets sorted array O(n) space; hash set during DFS O(n) space.

questions.title: Construct String from Binary Tree
questions.problem_statement: Construct string from binary tree: preorder with parentheses.
options: A) Recursion: if left null and right not null, add '()' for left
B) BFS with level tracking
C) Both A and string building
D) Use stack
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and string building
questions.explanation: Preorder: node.val + (left str) + (right str). Omit parentheses for left if null and right null.

questions.title: Diameter of Binary Tree
questions.problem_statement: Length of longest path between any two nodes (edges count).
options: A) For each node, compute leftHeight+rightHeight, track max
B) Height function returns height, updates global max
C) Both A and B
D) BFS from each leaf
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B
questions.explanation: DFS returns height, at each node update max = max(max, left+right).

questions.title: House Robber III (Binary Tree)
questions.problem_statement: Maximum sum of values without robbing directly connected nodes.
options: A) DFS returns (rob, notRob) for each node
B) Recursion: rob = node.val + left.notRob + right.notRob; notRob = max(left.rob,left.notRob)+max(right.rob,right.notRob)
C) Both A and B are same
D) Use memoization on node
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B are same
questions.explanation: Post-order DP: each node returns two values: max with current robbed and without.

questions.title: Redundant Connection (Union-Find)
questions.problem_statement: Undirected graph with n nodes and n edges; find edge to remove to make tree.
options: A) DFS to detect cycle, return last redundant edge
B) Union-find: if find(u) == find(v), edge is redundant
C) Both A and B, B is simpler
D) Topological sort
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B, B is simpler
questions.explanation: Union-find: iterate edges, if same root, redundant; else union.

questions.title: Redundant Connection II (Directed)
questions.problem_statement: Rooted tree with one extra edge; find the redundant edge.
options: A) Cases: node with two parents, or cycle detection
B) Union-find with indegree tracking
C) Both A and B together
D) DFS only
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B together
questions.explanation: Handle two-parent case: candidate edges; then detect cycle ignoring one candidate.

questions.title: Similar String Groups
questions.problem_statement: Group strings where strings are similar if differ by at most 2 positions.
options: A) Build graph O(n^2 * m) edges, union-find
B) For each string, generate all possible similar strings and map
C) Both A and B work, B faster if strings short
D) BFS on string graph
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, B faster if strings short
questions.explanation: Union-find: compare each pair O(n^2*m); optimization: generate neighbor strings by swapping.

questions.title: Accounts Merge
questions.problem_statement: Merge accounts with common emails.
options: A) Build graph of emails, DFS to find connected components
B) Union-find on emails, map email to account index
C) Both A and B work
D) Sort and group by name
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Union-find: union emails in same account, then group emails by root; map back to names.

questions.title: Evaluate Division
questions.problem_statement: Given equations a/b = value, evaluate queries.
options: A) Build weighted graph, DFS from a to b accumulating product
B) Union-find with weight (ratio) to root
C) Both A and B work
D) Floyd-Warshall
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Union-find: each node stores ratio to parent; find returns root and scaling factor.

questions.title: Satisfiability of Equality Equations
questions.problem_statement: Given equations like a==b and a!=b, check if satisfiable.
options: A) Union all == equations, then check != equations for same parent
B) Build graph for equality, check inequality in same component
C) Both A and B
D) Use BFS for equality graph
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B
questions.explanation: Union-find for equality; after union, check each inequality's endpoints are in different components.

questions.title: Most Stones Removed with Same Row or Column
questions.problem_statement: Stones on 2D plane, remove if share row/col with another stone. Max stones removed.
options: A) Each stone is node, connect if same row/col, answer = total - components
B) Union-find on row and column indices, count components
C) Both A and B, B uses row and col as separate nodes
D) DFS on stones
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B, B uses row and col as separate nodes
questions.explanation: Treat rows and columns as nodes; stone connects row and col; components = unique rows+cols - stones? formula: answer = stones - components.

questions.title: Number of Operations to Make Network Connected
questions.problem_statement: Find minimum cables to rearrange to connect all computers.
options: A) Count connected components, need components-1 cables
B) Also need enough spare cables (edges >= n-1)
C) Both A and B
D) Check DFS connectivity
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B
questions.explanation: If edges < n-1, return -1; else return components-1.

questions.title: Smallest String With Swaps
questions.problem_statement: You can swap pairs of indices given. Return lexicographically smallest string.
options: A) Union-find on indices, each component sort characters and assign back
B) DFS per component, collect indices and chars, sort, reassign
C) Both A and B work
D) Greedy per index
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work
questions.explanation: Indices in same component can be rearranged arbitrarily; sort each component's characters and place back.

questions.title: Regions Cut By Slashes
questions.problem_statement: Grid with ' /', '\\', ' '. Count regions.
options: A) Scale grid by 3x3, treat each cell as small squares, union adjacent
B) Union-find on 4 triangles per cell
C) Both A and B work, B is standard
D) DFS on expanded grid
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B work, B is standard
questions.explanation: Each cell divided into 4 triangles (0:up,1:right,2:down,3:left) and union based on slash.

questions.title: Minimum Cost to Connect Sticks (LeetCode Premium - concept)
questions.problem_statement: Combine sticks with cost = sum of lengths; min total cost.
options: A) Sort and always combine smallest two (min-heap)
B) Always combine largest two fails
C) Both A and greedy heap
D) DP like matrix chain
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and greedy heap
questions.explanation: Classic Huffman coding: min-heap, pop two, push sum, accumulate cost.

questions.title: Last Stone Weight
questions.problem_statement: Smash two largest stones, return last stone weight.
options: A) Sort each time O(n^2 log n)
B) Use max-heap (negatives in min-heap) O(n log n)
C) Both A and B, B is efficient
D) Use bucket sort
questions.correct_answer: C) Both A and B, B is efficient
questions.explanation: Push all into max-heap, pop two, push back difference.

questions.title: K Closest Points to Origin
questions.problem_statement: Find k closest points to origin (0,0).
options: A) Compute distances, sort O(n log n)
B) Min-heap of size k (or max-heap of size k derived)
C) Quick select O(n) average
D) All of the above
questions.correct_answer: D) All of the above
questions.explanation: Max-heap of size k stores k smallest distances; quick select finds k-th smallest distance.
